285. Emergence and evolution of ATM networks in the UK, 1967–2000

Business History 2009-01-01 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2009-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076790802602164
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Bernardo Bátiz‐LazoUniversity of Leicester; a School of Management, University of Leicester , UK; School of Management, University of Leicester , UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Research in this article traces the origins of a process of competitive change in British retail financial markets by looking at the emergence of cash dispensers technology, how it transformed into automated teller machines (ATMs) and how proprietary ATM networks gave way to total interoperability of cash withdrawals through a single common switch. Cash dispensers were an industry-specific innovation developed by British manufacturers (e.g. Chubb and De La Rue) which were, in turn, overtaken by US (e.g. NCR) and German (e.g. Siemens-Wincor) manufacturers. However, as the ATM became a global technology some of the leading providers (i.e. Burroughs, IBM and NCR) kept manufacturing and even their main design facilities in Scotland. The evolution of this technology illustrates changing boundaries of the banking organisation, the challenges faced by financial intermediaries to adopt on-line, real-time computing and highlights the role of network externalities in financial markets. From a business history perspective, the ATM, electronic funds transfer and other retail payment media have largely been neglected by British historians and management scholars. Yet the success of automated cash dispensers as a distribution channel in retail banking epitomises a shift in bank strategy, namely how applications of computer technology moved from being potential sources of competitive advantage to being a minimum requirement for effective competition in retail finance. This article thus promotes the idea that the history of technology must consider its users, their strategies and business models inasmuch as business histories of the late twentieth century will be incomplete without attention to developments in information and communications technologies.

KeywordsItaly: Economic History and Contemporary Issues; Digital Platforms and Economics; Competition (biology); Cash; IBM; Business history; Payment; Intermediary; Business; Commerce; Cash management; Competitive advantage; Finance; Economics; Marketing; Industrial organization; Management; Materials science; Nanotechnology; Ecology; Biology

290. Introduction

The Business History Review 2009-01-01 Available
JournalThe Business History Review
Publication date2009-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1017/s0007680500000179
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Per H. HansenCopenhagen Business School
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This issue of Business History Review is devoted to financial crisis, business failure, and scandal. As readers of this journal know, white-collar fraud is not a new phenomenon. Public and private authorities have confronted the problem for a long time, looking for ways to deal with it that will not stifle economic growth.

295. The internationalisation process theory and the internationalisation of Norwegian firms, 1945 to 1980

Business History 2009-05-01 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2009-05-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076790902844054
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Rolv Petter AmdamBI Norwegian Business School; a BI Norwegian School of Management , Oslo, Norway; BI Norwegian School of Management , Oslo, Norway
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

According to the internationalisation process theory firms tend to invest and expand in countries with a short psychic distance to the home country. This paper discusses the usefulness of bringing this theory into business history by analysing the internationalisation of Norwegian firms before the 1980s. The empirical contribution of this paper is that it adds new knowledge to our understanding of the early internationalisation of Norwegians firms. The theoretical contribution is that the paper develops the discussion on the usefulness of bringing networks into the internationalisation process theory. Based on the Norwegian case, there seems to be a need for including personal networks as one dimension of the psychic distance concept, not only in the new economy but also in the old economy.

KeywordsInternational Business and FDI; Global trade and economics; Innovation and Knowledge Management; Internationalization; Norwegian; Process (computing); Dimension (graph theory); Business; Business history; Process theory; Psychic; Industrial organization; Economics; Marketing; International trade; Management; Work in process; Computer science; Linguistics; Pathology; Philosophy; Pure mathematics; Alternative medicine; Operating system; Mathematics; Medicine

Matched fieldsabstract

300. The Corporate Pyramid Fable

The Business History Review 2010-01-01 Available
JournalThe Business History Review
Publication date2010-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1017/s0007680500002191
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Steven A. BankUniversity of California, Los Angeles; University of Cambridge; University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA) - School of Law
Brian R. CheffinsUniversity of Cambridge; University of Cambridge - Faculty of Law
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Although corporate pyramids are currently commonplace world-wide and although there have been “noteworthy pyramiders” in American business history, this controversial form of corporate organization is now a rarity in the United States. The conventional wisdom is that corporate pyramids disappeared in the U.S. when New Deal policymakers began taxing dividends paid to corporate shareholders. This version of events is more fable than truth. The introduction of the intercorporate dividend tax did not foster a rapid dismantling of corporate pyramids. Instead, pyramidal arrangements were already rare in the U.S., other than in the utilities sector, and the demise of utility pyramids was prompted by the Public Util- ities Holding Company Act of 1935 rather than by tax reform.

KeywordsCorporate Finance and Governance; Fable; Shareholder; Commission; Corporate law; Business; Market for corporate control; Corporate structure; Government (linguistics); Market economy; Dividend; Corporate tax; Corporate group; Corporate governance; Accounting; Economics; Finance; Double taxation; Tax avoidance; Linguistics; Philosophy; Art; Literature
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

301. A mark of distinction: Branding and trade mark law in the UK from the 1860s

Business History 2010-02-01 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2010-02-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076790903281033
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
John MercerQueen Mary University of London; Queen Mary, University of London; Queen Mary, University of London , UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

The development of branding is a neglected theme in business history. This article examines the emergence on a large scale of the unique product brand name – distinct from a company name or product descriptor – in the UK in the later nineteenth century. It looks at the interaction of branding strategies and UK trade mark law, which is shown to have accorded property rights in word-based marks only gradually and shaped the development of branding in the UK. Trademark application data from the 1870s to the 1920s is cited to illustrate the widespread take-up of the brand name in the UK from the 1880s, and to consider its use by different types of consumer goods firms. The article then analyses the effects of such branding into the twentieth century, including its contribution to competitive advantage, the introduction of brand architecture, and the problem of brand genericisation. It is argued that the adoption of the brand name marked a major shift in brands, from descriptions of origin to objects of artifice.

KeywordsAmerican History and Culture; Wine Industry and Tourism; Fashion and Cultural Textiles; Trademark; Product (mathematics); Theme (computing); Business history; Brand names; Brand management; Corporate branding; Business; Advertising; Brand extension; Economics; Marketing; Law; Political science; Management; Operating system; Computer science; Mathematics; Geometry
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

303. Chandler's Living History:<i>The Visible Hand</i>of Vertical Integration in Nineteenth Century America Viewed Under a Twenty‐First Century Transaction Costs Economics Lens

Journal of Management Studies 2010-02-09 Available
JournalJournal of Management Studies
Publication date2010-02-09
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-6486.2010.00927.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Marcelo BucheliUniversity of Illinois Urbana-Champaign; University of Illinois at Urbana–Champaign;; University of Illinois at Urbana–Champaign
Joseph T. MahoneyUniversity of Illinois Urbana-Champaign; University of Illinois at Urbana–Champaign;; University of Illinois at Urbana–Champaign
Paul M. VaalerUniversity of Minnesota System; University of Minnesota
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

abstract Alfred Chandler's recent passing is cause to review and celebrate his many contributions to business history. It also presents an opportunity to highlight links between his rich historical analyses concerning organizational and industrial innovation and contemporary management studies of the firm and industrial organization. We illustrate this point by applying transaction costs theory to several case studies from his 1977 masterwork narrating the emergence of vertically‐integrated firms in nineteenth‐century America, The Visible Hand . Vertical integration, organizational control, and innovation in manufacturing at McCormick Harvester and Singer Sewing Machines, and in transportation and distribution at Swift and United Fruit reflect managerial responses to classic transaction costs considerations including commercial relationships requiring the creation of specialized equipment and knowledge. Transaction costs analysis provides complementary historical insight on organizational innovation at these and other firms in the nineteenth century, and suggests when and where we might expect vertical integration strategies in emerging industries of the twenty‐first century.

KeywordsAccounting and Organizational Management; Management Theory and Practice; Economic Theory and Institutions; Transaction cost; Vertical integration; Database transaction; Point (geometry); Industrial organization; Business history; Distribution (mathematics); Economics; Business; Management; Computer science; Finance; Mathematical analysis; Geometry; Programming language; Mathematics
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

304. Enhancing Industry Association Theory: A Comparative Business History Contribution

Journal of Management Studies 2010-02-09 Available
JournalJournal of Management Studies
Publication date2010-02-09
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-6486.2010.00926.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
James ReveleyUniversity of Wollongong
Simon VilleUniversity of Wollongong
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

abstract Our comparative business historical examination of industry associations aims to enrich the under‐theorized study of this distinctive type of meta‐organization. We compare two New Zealand industry associations operating in the same supply chain but with differing degrees of associative capacity and types of external architecture. Our analysis of these associations builds on two strands of theory that rarely communicate with each other: New Institutional Economics (NIE) and Organizational–Institutional Theory (OIT). We demonstrate how NIE describes the structural potentialities for associational strength, while OIT addresses the relational context within associations. In turn, NIE's examination of external influences reinforces OIT suggestions that associations which are rich in social capital can become developmental in orientation. Our historical analysis supplies fresh theoretical insights into industry associations, thereby addressing conceptual issues of interest to management scholars who study bridging‐type organizations. On this basis, we argue that business history and organization studies complement each other.

KeywordsManagement and Organizational Studies; Cooperative Studies and Economics; Labor Movements and Unions; Association (psychology); Context (archaeology); Business history; Sociology; Positive economics; Marketing; Business; Management; Epistemology; Economics; History; Philosophy; Archaeology
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldstitle

305. Guest Editors' Introduction

Journal of Management Studies 2010-02-09 Available
JournalJournal of Management Studies
Publication date2010-02-09
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-6486.2010.00923.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Mary O’SullivanCalifornia University of Pennsylvania; University of Pennsylvania; University of Pennsylvania;; University of Pennsylvania - The Wharton School
Margaret B. W. GrahamMcGill University
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

abstract There are already manifold interactions between business history and management studies but, to date, they have tended to be more particular and patchy than general and systematic. In this introduction we summarize the arguments that scholars from business history, management studies and social science have made for closer contact between these fields which emphasize the benefits to be gained both for theoretical and historical research on business. We highlight how the articles published in this Special Issue show the wide variety of intellectual purposes, approaches, and benefits that closer engagement between business history and management theories might entail. Yet, concerns have also been raised about the value of a closer alliance between business history and management studies and we suggest that they are worthy of further consideration and discussion. To that end, we propose a more deliberate conversation about what it is we do as historians and theorists of business and what we seek to accomplish. Such a discussion would help us not only to better understand the limits to fruitful integration but also how to work together more productively in the collaborations that seem most worthwhile.

KeywordsManagement and Organizational Studies; Historical Economic and Social Studies; Management Theory and Practice; Variety (cybernetics); Conversation; Value (mathematics); Business history; Work (physics); Sociology; Alliance; Organization studies; Engineering ethics; Epistemology; Public relations; Political science; Management; Computer science; Economics; Engineering; Law; Mechanical engineering; Artificial intelligence; Machine learning; Communication; Philosophy
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

307. Renault in Spain: From assembly to manufacture, 1961–72

Business History 2010-06-01 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2010-06-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791003721985
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
T. Fernández de SevillaBarcelona School of Economics; Universitat de Barcelona; a Department of Economic History and Institutions , Universitat de Barcelona , Barcelona, Spain; Department of Economic History and Institutions , Universitat de Barcelona , Barcelona, Spain
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Two broad themes emerge from an analysis of the business history of Fabricacion de Automoviles Sociedad Anonima (FASA) (FASA-Renault from 1965) during the period 1961 to 1972. The first concerns the factors that enabled a less-industrialised country like Spain to develop a powerful car industry. The hypothesis analysed here, which follows Ha-Joon Chang and Paul Krugman, attributes the development to strategic industrial policies that were protectionist in nature. The second broad theme is that business strategies lay behind FASA's ability to adapt successfully to its environment and grow into a large company. The hypothesis examined here is that the company's success was a result of its ability to acquire the five capabilities to which Alfred D. Chandler attributes industrial success in the Second Technological Revolution.

KeywordsAsian Industrial and Economic Development; History, Culture, and Society; Communism, Protests, Social Movements; Protectionism; Theme (computing); Industrial Revolution; Business history; Business; Economy; Political science; Economic history; History; Economics; International trade; Computer science; Law; Operating system
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

308. Can macro-economic sources be used to define UK business performance, 1855–1914?

Business History 2010-07-01 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2010-07-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791003753160
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
A.J. ArnoldUniversity of Leicester; a School of Management, University of Leicester , Leicester, UK; School of Management, University of Leicester , Leicester, UK
Sean McCartneyLondon School of Business and Management; Queen Mary University of London; b School of Business and Management, Queen Mary, University of London , London, UK; School of Business and Management, Queen Mary, University of London , London, UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

The measurement of business performance addresses issues central to business history and this paper examines the evidence on rates of return for UK risk-bearing capital across the period 1855-1914. Existing series, based on the archival records of individual companies and on market data, provide results that are relatively reliable, if limited in scope. The national income accounting framework, in conjunction with capital stock measurements, instead offers the potential to identify a comprehensive series, although one subject to a number of valuation assumptions. In this paper, such a new series is provided and evaluated.

KeywordsHistorical Economic and Social Studies; Accounting and Organizational Management; Queen (butterfly); Business history; Macro; Business management; Management; Economic history; Sociology; Economics; Business; Business administration; Computer science; Botany; Hymenoptera; Biology; Programming language
Matched conceptsBusiness History

309. Learning the wrong lessons from history: Underestimating strategic change in business turnarounds

Business History 2010-07-01 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2010-07-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791003753186
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Andrew WildUniversity of Nottingham; a Nottingham University Business School , Nottingham, UK; Nottingham University Business School, Nottingham, UK;
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Firms that are able to shift their trajectory from continual failure to sustained success are rare, yet turnarounds do occur and are part of the business landscape. Little progress has been made over the last two decades in discovering the key factors that can lead to firms escaping from continual failure. This study examines turnarounds in the late twentieth century, finding that the importance of strategic change may have been significantly underestimated in previous turnaround research. Emphasis is given to the care that must be taken when constructing research methodologies to ensure we avoid learning the wrong lessons from history.

KeywordsCorporate Insolvency and Governance; Corporate Finance and Governance; Insurance and Financial Risk Management; Profitability index; Business history; Management; Accounting; Economics; Economic history; Marketing; Classical economics; Sociology; Business; Finance
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldskeywords

311. Teaching History in Business Schools: An Insider's View

Academy of Management Learning and Education 2010-12-01 Available
JournalAcademy of Management Learning and Education
Publication date2010-12-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.5465/amle.9.4.zqr697
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Robert E. WrightAugustana College
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

I question Madansky's (2008) critique of stand-alone business history courses by arguing that the subprime mortgage crisis of 2007 and the financial panic of 2008 may not have occurred if business history was taught more widely and thoroughly in business and public policy schools. The current crisis is the seventh of its kind in U.S. history and occurred for the same reason, perverse incentives, as the first six. History can also illuminate the best way out of the crisis and reduce the likelihood of recurrence.

KeywordsAmerican History and Culture; Management and Organizational Studies; Insider; Business history; Incentive; Financial crisis; Subprime crisis; Economics; Business; Public relations; Sociology; Management; Political science; Keynesian economics; Market economy; Law

313. Wayward agents, dominant elite, or reflection of internal diversity? A critique of Folkman, Froud, Johal and Williams on financialisation and financial intermediaries

Business History 2010-12-01 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2010-12-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2010.523458
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Geoffrey WoodUniversity of Sheffield; a School of Management , University of Sheffield , Sheffield, UK; School of Management , University of Sheffield , Sheffield , UK
Mike WrightErasmus University Rotterdam; University of Nottingham; b Centre for Management Buy-out Research , Nottingham University Business School , UK; c Rotterdam School of Management, Erasmus University , Rotterdam, The Netherlands; Centre for Management Buy-out Research , Nottingham University Business School , UK ; Rotterdam School of Management, Erasmus University , Rotterdam, The Netherlands
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Based on their earlier work on managerial capitalism and the literature on financialisation, P. Folkman, J. Froud, S. Johal, and K. Williams (2007, ‘Working for themselves: Financial intermediaries and present day capitalism’, Business History, 49(4), 552–572) argue that the rise of capital market intermediaries has both eroded traditional managerial power, and constitutes a powerful interest grouping with a distinct agenda that has a vested interest in permanent corporate restructuring and redistribution away from traditional stakeholders in the firm and, ultimately, shareholders as well. This paper critically evaluates these assumptions and conclusions. It specifically critiques the underlying assumptions of the Folkman et al. paper, and explores its relevance to understanding the changing relationship between stakeholders, and, indeed, the 2008 financial crisis.

KeywordsHousing, Finance, and Neoliberalism; Banking stability, regulation, efficiency; Economic Theory and Policy; Elite; Intermediary; Diversity (politics); Financial intermediary; Reflection (computer programming); Economics; Business; Market economy; Political economy; Finance; Sociology; Political science; Politics; Law; Anthropology; Programming language; Computer science
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

314. A Brief Postwar History of U.S. Consumer Finance

The Business History Review 2011-01-01 Available
JournalThe Business History Review
Publication date2011-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1017/s0007680511000778
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Andrea Ryannot available
Gunnar Trumbullnot available
Peter Tufanonot available
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

In this brief history of U.S. consumer finance since World War II, the sector is defined based on the functions delivered by firms in the form of payments, savings and investing, borrowing, managing risk, and providing advice. Evidence of major trends in consumption, savings, and borrowing is drawn from time-series studies. An examination of consumer decisions, changes in regulation, and business practices identifies four major themes that characterized the consumer finance sector: innovation that increased the choices available to consumers; enhanced access in the form of consumers' broadening participation in financial activities; do-it-yourself consumer finance, which both allowed and forced consumers to take greater responsibility for their own financial lives; and a resultant increase in household risk taking.

KeywordsFinancial Literacy, Pension, Retirement Analysis; Housing, Finance, and Neoliberalism; Housing Market and Economics; Consumption (sociology); Finance; Payment; Consumer spending; Economics; Business history; Business; Financial services; Consumer protection; Structured finance; Marketing; Commerce; Financial crisis; Macroeconomics; Management; Sociology; Social science; Recession

Author & affiliation

Christos PitelisBrunel University of London; Brunel University London#TAB#
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Edith Penrose's work on the multinational enterprise and the political economy of globalization and development is assessed as it relates to her views on business history. This essay was written on the occasion of the fiftieth anniversary of Penrose's classic 1959 book and her 1960 prize-winning paper on Hercules Powder, which was published in the Review . Penrose came close to providing a theory of “internalization,” compared foreign direct investment to market-type contracting relations, and even discussed transaction costs–related arguments. However, she largely accepted the existence of fi rms and did not examine why firms exist vis-à-vis alternatives, such as markets. Her views on the political economy of globalization, relations between multinational enterprise and the state, and development have proved to be incisive, mostly accurate, and ahead of their time.

KeywordsPolitical Economy and Marxism; Labor Movements and Unions; Economic Theory and Policy; Globalization; Multinational corporation; Foreign direct investment; Coase theorem; Business history; Politics; Leverage (statistics); Economics; Transaction cost; Neoclassical economics; Economy; Political science; Political economy; Market economy; Law; Management; Finance; Machine learning; Computer science
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

318. The Origin and Development of Markets: A Business History Perspective

The Business History Review 2011-01-01 Available
JournalThe Business History Review
Publication date2011-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1017/s0007680511000018
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Mark Cassonnot available
John S. Leenot available
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

The origins of the market are obscure, but substantial documentary evidence survives from the eleventh century onward, when chartered markets and new towns were established across Western Europe. The expansion of the market system is important for business history because it created new opportunities for business growth. There has been no systematic literature review on market evolution since Henri Pirenne and Raymond de Roover, and this article attempts to fill the gap. It shows that successful markets were regulated–often by civic authorities–to maintain a reputation for reasonable prices and quality control. Markets were located at both transport hubs and centers of consumption, even when the latter were quite remote. However, as transport and communication costs declined, shakeouts occurred and only the larger markets survived.

KeywordsHistorical Economic and Social Studies; Historical Economic and Legal Thought; Reformation and Early Modern Christianity; Reputation; Business history; Eleventh; Consumption (sociology); Perspective (graphical); Quality (philosophy); Control (management); Market economy; Market development; Business; Economics; Economy; Economic history; Political science; Law; Management; Sociology; Social science; Physics; Philosophy; Artificial intelligence; Computer science; Epistemology; Acoustics

Matched fieldstitle

319. Explaining corporate success: The structure and performance of British firms, 1950–84

Business History 2011-02-01 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2011-02-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2011.546668
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
David M. HigginsUniversity of York; a The York Management School , University of York , York, UK; The York Management School , University of York , York, UK
Steven A. TomsUniversity of York; a The York Management School , University of York , York, UK; The York Management School , University of York , York, UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Predictions from dominant strands of the management strategy and business history literature suggest that the adoption of the multi-divisional form is associated with corporate success. There is theoretical support for this contention and, in certain non-British contexts and historical periods, also some confirmatory evidence. To examine the relationship in the British case, this article examines the strategy and structure characteristics of successful firms between 1950 and 1984. To do so it utilises an extensive accounting database to compute the return on capital employed for all quoted companies in the period. Using this measure, and applying it to a sub-sample of long-run surviving companies, it produces a ranking of firms according to profitability. A sample of best performing firms is matched to a paired sample of firms selected from the bottom of the financial performance ranking, and their organisation structures are contrasted. Examples are used to illustrate cases where strategies have been well supported by the structures adopted and have successful financial performance outcomes. A tendency for firms to adopt holding company structures in preference to the multi-divisional form is identified, particularly before 1970. Transitions from the functional to the holding company form tend to be successful in general and appear more successful than transitions to the multi-divisional form, again in the earlier decades in particular. These findings cast doubt on the universal applicability of the Chandler–Williamson model of the large, professionally managed, multi-divisional enterprise.

KeywordsCorporate Finance and Governance; Accounting and Organizational Management; Organizational Downsizing and Restructuring; Ranking (information retrieval); Profitability index; Sample (material); Capital structure; Accounting; Preference; Business; Marketing; Economics; Industrial organization; Finance; Microeconomics; Computer science; Debt; Chromatography; Machine learning; Chemistry
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This paper is the introduction to the Business History special issue on European Business Models. The volume presents results of the international project about mapping European corporations, within the strategy, structure, ownership and performance (SSOP) framework. The paper describes the historical developments of the SSOP framework and introduces the contributions to the special issue.

321. The Relevant Past: Why the History of Management Should Be Critical for Our Future

Academy of Management Learning and Education 2011-03-01 Available
JournalAcademy of Management Learning and Education
Publication date2011-03-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.5465/amle.10.1.zqr77
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Stephen CummingsVictoria University of Wellington
Todd BridgmanVictoria University of Wellington
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

When history is covered in business schools, its simplistic and evolutionary treatment goes largely unquestioned by instructors and students. To demonstrate, we show the representation of Max Weber in management texts to be dubious, a reflection of a peculiar perspective which is driven by a desire to justify the latest management ideas. However, by encouraging students to develop an ability to think critically about historical representations such as these, not only do we foster the benefits others have attributed to a greater historical awareness, we also encourage students to be more creative management thinkers for the future.

KeywordsManagement and Organizational Studies; Management Theory and Practice; Accounting and Organizational Management; Perspective (graphical); Critical management studies; Business history; Sociology; Critical reflection; Representation (politics); Epistemology; Reflection (computer programming); Engineering ethics; Psychology; Social science; Management; Political science; Pedagogy; Computer science; Law; Engineering; Philosophy; Economics; Politics; Programming language; Artificial intelligence

322. Italian fashion business: Achievements and challenges (1970s–2000s)

Business History 2011-06-01 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2011-06-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2011.565512
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Elisabetta MerloBocconi University; a Department of Institutional Analysis and Public Management , Università Bocconi Milan , Milan, Italy; b Centre for Research on Entrepreneurship and Entrepreneurs , Università Bocconi Milan , Milan, Italy
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

The article aims to provide a critical insight into the history of the Italian fashion business from the 1970s onwards by exploring a mix of sources including information provided by the industrial partners of fashion designers, articles published in the economic and financial press, and evidence collected from the main financial and economic databases. The analysis shows that although Italian ready-to-wear was extremely effective at meeting the new trends emerging in consumption patterns, Italian fashion companies have remained niche businesses compared to their French counterparts. The conclusions stress the contribution which business history can provide in explaining such a gap as well as the strategies pursued by fashion corporations.

KeywordsFashion and Cultural Textiles; Cultural Industries and Urban Development; Art History and Market Analysis; Business history; Consumption (sociology); Business; Marketing; Economy; Economics; Management; Sociology; Social science

323. The English cotton spinning industry, 1780–1840, as revealed in the columns of the <i>London Gazette</i>

Business History 2011-06-01 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2011-06-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2011.565510
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Peter M. SolarUCLouvain Saint-Louis Brussels; Vrije Universiteit Brussel; a Vesalius College , Vrije Universiteit Brussel , Brussels, Belgium; b Facultés universitaires Saint-Louis , Brussels, Belgium; Vesalius College , Vrije Universiteit Brussel , Brussels, Belgium
John S. LyonsMiami University; c Department of Economics , Miami University , Oxford, OH, USA; Department of Economics , Miami University , Oxford, OH, USA
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

We investigate the early development of English cotton spinning by analysing about 700 bankruptcies and 1300 dissolutions of partnership reported in the London Gazette, 1770–1840. The data show three temporal cycles, peaking in the early to mid-1800s, in the later 1820s and again in the later 1830s, near the ends of investment booms. Both earlier peaks were absolutely higher than the later ones, despite industry expansion. Over time both bankruptcies and dissolutions show rising concentration of spinning in greater Lancashire, and within greater Lancashire in the surrounding towns rather than in Manchester. The industry was throughout dominated by single proprietors or firms with only two partners. Integration with weaving was increasing steadily. The paper demonstrates the potential of the Gazette, now searchable online, as a source for business and industrial history.

KeywordsHistorical Economic and Social Studies; Boom; Investment (military); Industrial Revolution; Weaving; Business history; Economic history; General partnership; Economics; Economy; History; Business; Commerce; Political science; Law; Engineering; Finance; Archaeology; Mechanical engineering; Politics; Environmental engineering
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldskeywords

324. Varieties of business history: Subject and methods for the twenty-first century

Business History 2011-06-01 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2011-06-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2011.565515
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Christopher KobrakESCP Business School; University of Oxford; a ESCP Europe , Paris, France; b Centre for Corporate Reputation , University of Oxford , Oxford, UK; Centre for Corporate Reputation , University of Oxford , Oxford, UK; ESCP Europe , Paris, France
Andrea Schneiderc GUG , Frankfurt, Germany; GUG , Frankfurt, Germany
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This paper deals with different approaches to business history. It argues that conflicting choices about methodology and subject can enrich a discipline, but that some of the current disputes among business historians produce unnecessary opportunity costs and block a more integrated understanding of how firms function in their larger social, political and economic contexts. The paper provides examples of how the separation in the field works against writing business history that is at once rigorous and appeals to broad audiences. It also suggests two approaches for bridging methodological differences. The first calls for reviving some basic historiographical notions. The second involves developing a closer relationship with business to gain more access to private, primary source materials. German experiences are drawn on to show how mutually beneficial academic–business cooperation can be.

KeywordsHistorical Economic and Social Studies; European Political History Analysis; Business history; Historiography; German; Bridging (networking); Politics; Subject (documents); Field (mathematics); Function (biology); Sociology; Public relations; Economics; Political science; Management; Computer science; Law; History; Library science; Pure mathematics; Archaeology; Evolutionary biology; Mathematics; Computer network; Biology

325. Regulating and deregulating the public utilities 1830–2010

Business History 2011-08-01 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2011-08-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2011.599592
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Judith CliftonUniversidad de Cantabria; a Facultad de Ciencias Económicas y Empresariales , Universidad de Cantabria , Santander, Spain
Pierre LanthierUniversité du Québec à Trois-Rivières; b Département des sciences humaines , Université du Québec à Trois-Rivières , Trois-Rivières, Canada
Harm G. SchröterUniversity of Bergen; c Department of Archaeology, History, Cultural Studies and Religion , University of Bergen , Bergen, Norway; Department of Archaeology, History, Cultural Studies and Religion , University of Bergen , Bergen, Norway
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

History can provide invaluable insights into important issues of the economic and social regulation of utilities, and offer lessons towards future debates. But the history of utility regulation – which speaks of changing, diverse and complex experiences around the world – was, unfortunately, sidelined or marginalised when economists and policymakers enthusiastically embraced the question of how to reform the utilities from the 1970s. This paper provides an overview of the three, overarching, `waves' of utility regulation from the nineteenth century to the present, documenting how, when and why the ways in which the roles of the state, the market and firms altered over time. It then contextualises and explains the main contributions of each of the papers included in this special issue of Business History, which cover energy, communications, water, transportation and other urban infrastructure regulation, across Western Europe, the United States and Australia.

KeywordsPolitical and Economic history of UK and US; American Environmental and Regional History; Water Governance and Infrastructure; State (computer science); Business history; Political science; Economics; Political economy; Public administration; Management; Computer science; Algorithm

Matched fieldsabstract

326. Management Research in the UK: A Personal View

British Journal of Management 2011-08-19 Available
JournalBritish Journal of Management
Publication date2011-08-19
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-8551.2011.00768.x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Cary L. CooperLancaster University; Lancaster University Ghana; Lancaster University Management School, University of Lancaster, Lancaster LA1 4YW, UKEmail: [email protected]; Lancaster University Management School, University of Lancaster, Lancaster LA1 4YW, UK Email: [email removed]
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Thomas Jefferson once reflected that ‘the past is prologue’, so to understand the current state and future development of management research in the UK we have to briefly explore its origins. The academic field of management in UK higher education has a very recent history, in contrast with many other academic disciplines. Indeed, the first academic presence of management in the UK was only in l917, at the seat of the industrial revolution, Manchester. The Manchester Mechanics Institution established the Department of Industrial Administration, whose role was specifically to train engineers and scientists for potential future managerial roles, with little if any research infrastructure or strategy. This Institute metamorphosed into the University of Manchester Institute of Science and Technology in 1966 (as an autonomous body but also as a Faculty of the University of Manchester), at around the same time as the Foundation of Management Education was established to create two UK centres of excellence in management education, the London Business School and the Manchester Business School – both created through private sector funding in 1964. From 1964 onwards, the number and range of academic development in the management sciences escalated to the point that by the 2009 Research Assessment Exercise (RAE) there were over 100 business and management schools in higher education institutes, producing thousands of undergraduates, MBAs and PhDs. The field went through what Preston and Post (1974) described as the three stages of managerial development, which they referred to as the ‘three managerial revolutions’. The first managerial revolution was the appearance of management itself as a specialized function within hierarchical organizations. The second revolution, ‘professionalization’, was encouraged by the growth in industrial organizations and the complexity of managerial tasks. The third, ‘participation’, was ‘the inclusion of persons and groups involved and concerned with the diverse outcomes of managerial activity as participants in the managerial process’. It is this third revolution that led to the importance of research, particularly in systematically evaluating what worked and what did not work in this managerial process. There has been a longer history of research in a limited number of subjects allied and/or integral to management, such as economics, finance, occupational psychology, operational research and the like, but as new functional areas of interest emerged in management (i.e. marketing, human resource management), research was given a new impetus, stimulating interdisciplinary work between disciplines and functions within management and between the management sciences and other social science disciplines. But the real sea change occurred with the advent of the first RAE in the 1980s. This process of rewarding universities for research output directly had the effect of encouraging universities to recruit and develop their researchers, and in the naturally competitive world of business schools it had an even more profound impact of galvanizing researchers to publish their work in the best international journals. There were not only the individual incentives in terms of career advancement through their research output, but also by enhancing their research portfolio it allowed them to be more attractive to higher rated business schools and/or universities – it gave them, in essence, greater career mobility. From the mid-1980s until the last RAE in 2009, the quantity and quality of research did improve enormously, with many British management academics now publishing in more highly ranked international journals and working more collaboratively with academics from other countries. I was fortunate enough to be a member of the RAE panel in management and business in 1991, and then Chair of this panel in 1996 and 2001. I could see how incentive-based approaches, like the RAE in the UK, could motivate both business schools and their academics to invest more effort in applying for research grants, working collaboratively with colleagues on communal research projects, taking greater risks in publishing in more highly rated journals, attending more high profile international conferences (e.g. Academy of Management annual conference) and advancing their special research interests in the wider global community. By the 2001 RAE in the UK, however, the research culture had been changed so dramatically that ‘the research ethic’ was now embedded in the psyche of management researchers, no longer needing the incentives of an RAE that in earlier years stimulated aspiring researchers. Indeed, by 2001 and beyond, game theory overtook the more positive aspects of the RAE, as universities tried to find ways of manufacturing good results (by the proportion of research active staff they put in, by creating joint authorships which were in name only etc.). So the RAE had achieved its goals by the turn of the millennium and in my view no longer served any useful purpose, even in equitably distributing government research funding for the higher education sector (with the science and technology subjects getting a disproportionate allocation compared with the social sciences, the arts and humanities). So the good news is that the UK's research base in the various management disciplines is vibrant, and now part of the DNA of management and business school academics. So, what research is taking place and being published in the UK, and has this changed over the last couple of decades? I tentatively explored this by looking at the output of the British Journal of Management (BJM) from 1990 to 1995 and comparing it with papers published in 2005–2010. I did a rough categorization of papers into a number of different functional areas like ‘marketing’ or ‘organizational behaviour’ etc.; or by research topics like ‘mergers and acquisitions’, ‘training and development’, ‘strategic change’ etc. It was interesting to note that at the beginning of the life of BJM there were a large number of papers that were in ‘the disciplines’ that make up management, particularly marketing, macro organizational behaviour, operations management, accounting and strategy, as opposed to research topic based papers. Incidentally, this was very different from the papers that appeared in the Academy of Management journals of the time, which tended to include papers on organizational behaviour, strategy, management learning etc. but rarely included papers in disciplines outside of organizational behaviour, strategy, human resource management etc., such are marketing, accounting and operations management. Where there were papers in BJM on specific topic areas (illustrated by the titles of some of the BJM papers below), they were in mergers and acquisitions (e.g. ‘The impact of mergers and acquisitions on people at work’), strategic change (e.g. ‘Patterns of strategic change in the development of human resource management’) and management training and development (e.g. ‘Managing as a performing art’), with a smaller number on gender (e.g. ‘Men managers and women workers: women employees as an under-used resource’), organizational psychology or micro-organizational behaviour (e.g. ‘The content of the psychological contract’), entrepreneurship (e g. ‘What does it mean to be enterprising’) and others in the general management arena (e.g. ‘What is happening to middle management’). When we look at the most recent five-year period, from 2005 to 2010, we notice a decline in published papers in some of the disciplines of the earlier years, particularly marketing, accounting, operations management and macro-organizational behaviour, which may indicate the influence of the Academy of Management and/or the internationalization of BJM and/or the editor of the journal during these different sampling periods. I suspect it is basically about the maturity of the academic field, as well as its international orientation, which have broadened the discipline base of published papers. We now see a wider distribution of disciplines from human resource management to micro-organizational behaviour to public sector management to organizational theory, as well as the fields that were more prominent over 20 years ago but less visibly dominating the journal pages. When it comes to the specific topics of interest, once again there is a more even distribution of subject areas, but with interest still in mergers and acquisition, strategic change and change management and gender, but with a more expansive portfolio of topics (illustrated by some of the titles of papers in that topic area) such as cross-cultural issues (e.g. ‘Promotability of host-country nationals: a cross-cultural study’), ethics (e.g. ‘Business ethics as practice’), top management (e.g. ‘Top management, team heterogeneity, strategic change and operational performance’), corporate reputation (e.g. ‘Examining the antecedents and consequences of corporate reputation: a customer perspective’), corporate social responsibility (e.g. ‘Employee volunteering and social capital: contributions to corporate social responsibility), knowledge networks (‘The role of non-structural and informal mechanisms of integration and coordination as forces in knowledge creation’) and innovation (e.g. ‘How innovative are UK firms’) but the most surprising significant addition to the literature, but probably in retrospect in reality the least surprising, was the large number of published studies on leadership (Avolio, Walumbwa and Weber, 2009). This was the single largest category of specific research topic areas, but it was also frequently linked to one of the other issues above, as illustrated by papers entitled ‘Phases of the adoption of innovation in organizations: effects of environment, organization and top managers’ or ‘Leadership and effectiveness in the context of gender: the role of leaders’ verbal behaviour’ etc. Leadership, at all levels in an organization, is an important aspect of achieving any of the above, whether in terms of ethics or corporate reputation or corporate social responsibility (Rothstein and Burke, 2010). Also, given the economic downturn and recession, the issue of leadership has come to the fore, particularly in the finance sector, the lack of leadership in managing risk and growth and the need for a different management style as we enter ‘recession recovery’ mode. As Gadman and Cooper (2009) suggest the leadership challenge is that in this era of overwhelming complexity and ambiguity, contemporary leadership practices are failing to respond to this new world order because they are based on outdated assumptions about the way the world works. These tenets, which emphasize ‘power over and control of’ people, have outlived their usefulness. To seize today's rich patterns of opportunities, we need trust and openness, shared information, clusters of capabilities and a focus on teaming these capabilities through creative leadership. For me it is really heartening to see topics emerging from the literature which have real meaning for the times we live in. The management research community are more and more concerned about leadership; about how we can use the knowledge base and technology we have to improve performance and get better work–life balance; about innovation and creativity; about ethics, given Enron, the expenses debacle of UK politicians and the like; about how we manage effectively strategic change, whilst minimizing its impact on the health and well-being of employees; and about what we all need to do to give back to the community through corporate social responsibility and sustainability more generally. This trend may be partly a result of the importance of ‘research impact’, and its implications for future management research funding, but I would like to think that business school academics see the significance of making their work relevant, whether it is in theory-building or in their empirical investigations, because they have become more interested in the implications of their work for policy making and ultimately seeing their work translated into strategic and significant effective change (Cooper and Starkey, 2010). Much of what is done in the name of management research can positively contribute to the economy and to enhanced organizational performance. Burke and Cooper (2008) highlight a number of examples of management research that can positively influence organizational performance and the success of an economy. Sirota, Mischkind and Meltzer (2005) found that companies who have more enthusiastic and job-satisfied employees outperform the average stock market prices of other companies in their sector by more than two and a half times; and that companies with low job satisfaction and unenthusiastic employees lag behind their industry competitors' average price by five times. The authors found that the role of management was fundamental to levels of employee satisfaction and enthusiasm. Managers who engendered pride, camaraderie and equity among their employees produced the best performance. Leslie, Loch and Schaninger (2006) also explored the relationship of management practices in 230 businesses around the world, collecting data on more than 115,000 individual managers and employees. They concluded that three management practices were common among many of their high performing organizations: clarifying roles and accountabilities, articulating a compelling direction and vision for the future, and developing a strong performance-oriented culture based on openness and trust. Kotter (1996) and Kotter and Cohen's (2002) follow-up work found that successful management practices for large-scale change, in over 100 cases of major organizational restructurings, identified eight successful managerial behaviours: (1) increasing urgency for change among respected and influential employees; (2) building the guiding team for the change, those with credibility, skills and reputations; (3) creating the right vision; (4) communicating to all stakeholders for buy-in; (5) empowering all to action; (6) creating short-term wins; (7) being persistent, showing employees that the vision is being realized; and (8) perpetuating the change, making the new behaviours become part of the new culture. And finally, the notion that major structural change inevitably means downsizing and outsourcing was contradicted by Cascio's (2002) research involving an 18-year study of the Standard and Poor's 500 firms, showing that companies who restructured through downsizing were not only less profitable than those who did not, but tended to do far worse. Treating people as disposable assets, to exploit them when you need them but to dispense with them when you do not, has adverse consequences, not only to the health and self-esteem of individuals but also to the bottom line of the firm. All of these studies are examples of how management research can make a difference to the performance, productivity and well-being of organizations. This is evidence-based management, with a more focused purpose to provide the data and support organizations need, particularly during the onerous economic times we are going through. Weitzel (2009) sets the challenge for peak performing organizations in the future: unrelenting pressure for continuing change from technological innovation, and altered expectations of clients and customers, force high performance leaders to guide their organizations through each new set of pressures for change. High performance leaders recognize they must work with those they lead to make them part of the process, as origins and not pawns, from the earliest stages, through the planning for change and then through the completion to the next phase. The high performing organization operates as a team or set of teams. Clearly innovation and genius are important ingredients. But success in a high performance culture is the result of the actions of all. We, as management researchers, are part of that team, providing the ideas, evidence and good practice examples, as ammunition for change and development in the managerial and organizational arena – a challenge, never needed as much as in today's world of business. Cary L. Cooper, CBE, is Distinguished Professor of Organizational Psychology and Health at Lancaster University Management School, Chair of the Academy of Social Sciences and Founding President of the British Academy of Management.

KeywordsManagement Theory and Practice; Management and Organizational Studies; Accounting and Organizational Management; Excellence; Management; Prologue; Business history; Institution; Higher education; Sociology; Industrial Revolution; Political science; Public relations; Engineering ethics; Engineering; Social science; Economics; History; Law; Archaeology
Matched conceptsBusiness History

327. Seaside tourism in Europe: Business, urban and comparative history

Business History 2011-10-01 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2011-10-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2011.590936
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
John K. WaltonUniversity of the Basque Country; Ikerbasque; a Department of Contemporary History , University of the Basque Country UPV/EHU , Leoia, Spain, IKERBASQUE, Basque Foundation for Science, Bilbao, Spain; Department of Contemporary History , University of the Basque Country UPV/EHU , Leoia, Spain, IKERBASQUE, Basque Foundation for Science, Bilbao, Spain
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This article explores aspects of the relationship between business history and urban history through a discussion of the seaside resort as a type of town that might also be regarded as a business (as might other kinds of town specialising in leisure and tourism). In the process it looks comparatively at aspects of the development of such towns across Europe, at the range of ways in which an understanding of seaside tourism contributes to a more satisfactory grasp of how businesses and societies function, and at the reasons for the enduringly marginal status of research in this sector and its limited integration into the perceived ‘mainstream' of all kinds of history, including business history.

330. Sensemaking, storytelling and the legitimization of elite business careers

Human Relations 2012-01-01 Available
JournalHuman Relations
Publication date2012-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1177/0018726711425616
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Mairi MacleanUniversity of Exeter; University of Exeter Business School, UK,
Charles HarveyNewcastle University; Newcastle University, UK,
Robert ChiaUniversity of Strathclyde; Strathclyde Business School, UK,
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This article examines elite business careers through the dual lens of sensemaking and storytelling as recounted in life-history interviews with business leaders. It explores how they make sense of, narrativize and legitimate their experiences of building their careers within and beyond large organizations. The research contribution is twofold. First, we explicate the sensemaking processes embedded within the multifarious stories recorded in life-history interviews, identified as locating, meaning-making and becoming. Second, we contribute to the literature on legitimacy by examining how business leaders use their storytelling as a vehicle for self-legitimization, (re)framing their accounts of their own success and justifying their position to themselves and others. In a world where reputations are hard won but easily lost, business leaders must nurture a life-history narrative which is socially desirable if their careers are to remain on track. This may serve them well through the creative evolution of their organizational journeys.

KeywordsManagement and Organizational Studies; Gender Diversity and Inequality; Corporate Identity and Reputation; Sensemaking; Storytelling; Framing (construction); Narrative; Legitimacy; Elite; Sociology; Public relations; Meaning-making; Narrative inquiry; Meaning (existential); Business history; Political science; Psychology; Management; Engineering; Law; Philosophy; Structural engineering; Economics; Psychotherapist; Linguistics; Politics
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldskeywords

331. Business History: A Cultural and Narrative Approach

The Business History Review 2012-01-01 Available
JournalThe Business History Review
Publication date2012-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1017/s0007680512001201
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Per H. HansenDepartment of Management, Politics and Philosophy
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This article argues that a cultural and narrative perspective can enrich the business history field, encourage new and different questions and answers, and provide new ways of thinking about methods and empirical material. It discusses what culture is and how it relates to narratives. Taking a cultural and narrative approach may affect questions, sources, and methodologies, as well as the status of our results. Finally, a narrative approach may contribute to our historical understanding of entrepreneurship and globalization.

KeywordsManagement and Organizational Studies; Wine Industry and Tourism; Management Theory and Practice; Narrative; Perspective (graphical); Narrative network; Sociology; Globalization; Field (mathematics); Affect (linguistics); Entrepreneurship; Narrative history; Narrative inquiry; Business history; Epistemology; Narrative criticism; History; Political science; Linguistics; Art; Visual arts; Philosophy; Communication; Archaeology; Law; Mathematics; Pure mathematics

333. Introduction: Behind the Brand

The Business History Review 2012-01-01 Available
JournalThe Business History Review
Publication date2012-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1017/s0007680512000384
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Teresa da Silva Lopesnot available
Paul Duguidnot available
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Once described as “neglected,” brands and brand history have benefited from a recent surge of work in business history, economics, and management studies. This research, however, remains relatively tightly focused. Most attention is given to successful, long-lived brands and to the entrepreneurs who developed them. As in the market, so in research, the attraction of successful brands is understandable. Nevertheless, such a focus only reinforces both the well-known tendency to read history through the eyes of the winners and the assumption that losers and also-rans have nothing to tell us. The authors of the articles in this special section share both the wish to look beyond such assumptions and the belief that one way to do so is by complementing the study of brands with the study of trademarks and of the related legal, economic, and business arrangements that stand behind brands and provide context for their development since the nineteenth century.

KeywordsAmerican History and Culture; Nothing; Context (archaeology); Business history; Brand names; Brand management; Advertising; Marketing; Business; Sociology; Economics; History; Management; Epistemology; Philosophy; Archaeology
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

334. “Forgotten Heroes and Forgotten Issues”: Business and Trademark History during the Nineteenth Century

The Business History Review 2012-01-01 Available
JournalThe Business History Review
Publication date2012-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1017/s0007680512000402
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
David M. Higginsnot available
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This reassessment of the importance of trademarks in business during the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries reveals that the focus by business historians on the beverage and processed-foodstuff industries has resulted in comparative neglect of the textile and metal-fabrication industries. The trademark histories of the latter two show that they followed their own paths, which resulted in their adopting three solutions to trademark issues that differed sharply from the approaches taken by the former two. The textile and metalfabrication sectors participated heavily in the evolution of an international regime to protect intellectual property; featured prominently in the development of patents in trademarks and trade names; and devised unique institutional solutions to the emerging problem of conflicting private marks in the Lancashire cotton-textile and Sheffield edge-tool industries. The history of these two industries indicates that trademark protection was not sufficient to ensure international competitiveness and long-run survival.

KeywordsAmerican History and Culture; Historical Studies and Socio-cultural Analysis; American Environmental and Regional History; Trademark; Intellectual property; Business history; Textile; Textile industry; Business; Economy; Commerce; Law; International trade; Economics; Political science; History; Economic history; Archaeology

336. H&amp;M – documenting the story of one of the world's largest fashion retailers

Business History 2012-02-01 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2012-02-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2011.617203
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Ingrid Giertz-MårtensonCentre for Business History , Stockholm , Sweden
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

The interest of fashion companies in documenting their history has been almost non-existent in Sweden until recently. For the Centre for Business History in Stockholm, the commission to secure the history of Sweden's largest fashion company and one of the world's largest fashion retailers, H&M, therefore offers new challenges. This study analyses the methods being used in this project. Apart from collecting, saving and digitising all existing documents from the company's 60 year-history, the documentation will be based on a considerable number of interviews with past and current employees. These oral history interviews will deal with a number of questions important to scholars doing contemporary business history.

KeywordsFashion and Cultural Textiles; Historical Gender and Feminism Studies; Cultural History and Identity Formation; Business history; Documentation; Oral history; Commission; World history; Business Review; Business; Marketing; History; Accounting; Management; Advertising; Political science; Economic history; Law; Economics; Finance; Computer science; Archaeology; Programming language

337. Madeleine Vionnet and<i>Galeries Lafayette</i>: The unlikely marriage of a Parisian couture house and a French department store, 1922–40

Business History 2012-02-01 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2012-02-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2011.617208
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Florence Brachet ChampsaurÉcole des hautes études en sciences sociales; Ecole des Hautes Etudes en Sciences Sociales (EHESS) and Galeries Lafayette , Paris , France
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

In the past, fashion history has traditionally produced monographs on talented designers emphasising the creativity of the luxury couture business and the tastes of its elite clientele. This case study, based on the unpublished records of Galeries Lafayette, offers a balanced and decompartmentalised interpretation of relationships among the players in the fashion system. Fashion designer Madeleine Vionnet never considered herself an artist and was well aware of the commercial aspects of the business, while the owner of Galeries Lafayette, Théophile Bader, tried to generate corporate synergy between the couture house and the department store. The examination of the partnership between Vionnet and Bader raises important questions, not only about counterfeiting but also about the transfer of creativity from designers to manufacturers.

339. Globalising the hotel industry 1946–68: A multinational case study of the Intercontinental Hotel Corporation

Business History 2012-02-21 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2012-02-21
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2011.631116
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Mary QuekUniversity of Hertfordshire; a Marketing &amp; Enterprise, Business School , University of Hertfordshire , de Havilland Campus, Hatfield , UK; Marketing &amp; Enterprise, Business School , University of Hertfordshire , de Havilland Campus, Hatfield , UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This article employs one case study, of Intercontinental Hotel Corporation, to examine the globalisation of the hotel industry between 1946 and 1968. The results show that the advent of multinational hotel development was characterised by the importance of government policies in shaping multinational expansion, whose trajectory was also closely related to the growth of the airline industry, underpinned by consumer demand change. This historical analysis illuminates capabilities specific to a firm as well as the entry mode and locations selected for international expansion as important factors in driving financial performance. Finally, this study contributes to the international business and business history literature by exploring international business development in the context of a small sample size and longitudinal approach.

KeywordsInternational Business and FDI; Wine Industry and Tourism; Business Strategy and Innovation; Multinational corporation; Corporation; Context (archaeology); Globalization; Business; International business; Hotel industry; Government (linguistics); Marketing; Industrial organization; Economics; Economy; Market economy; Management; Finance; Tourism; Political science; Paleontology; Law; Linguistics; Philosophy; Biology

343. Business history and operations management

Business History 2012-03-28 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2012-03-28
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2011.631121
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Niall PiercyUniversity of Bath; a School of Management , University of Bath , Bath , BA2 7AY , UK; School of Management, University of Bath, Bath BA2 7AY, UK#TAB#
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Operations management is a key function in the modern organisation and an important area of study in the business school. Like many subjects it remains separated from the business history community. The practice of operations management can gain meaningful and significant lessons from proper consideration of the historical antecedents of current practices. Unfortunately, more than any other business area, operations management has a habit of forgetting the lessons of the past and ‘reinventing the wheel’. The purpose of this paper is to emphasise the value of historical analysis in operations management, assess the level of historical coverage of the development of operations within that subject area (taking a review of OM textbooks as a proxy), and highlight the valuable opportunities for the business history community to engage with their operations colleagues to better guide the next generation of operations management education and practice.

KeywordsOperations Management Techniques; Accounting and Organizational Management; Organizational Management and Leadership; Business history; Business; Business management; Process management; Operations management; Management; Economics; Business administration
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldstitle

344. Business history, global networks and the future of mobility

Business History 2012-04-05 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2012-04-05
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2011.631126
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Colin DivallInstitute of Railway Studies & Transport History , York , UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Globalisation raises serious concerns about ecological sustainability and social equality. This article proposes that business historians should write a usable past that is framed by the imperative of addressing these issues. The transport and communication networks that underpin global trade and travel offer an opportunity to narrate such a past. In particular business enterprises have historically helped to shape the ways consumers think about and realise bodily movement. By contributing to a genealogy of these mobility cultures, business historians can help to uncover the mythic traces that continued to shape contemporary public and policy understandings of global mobility.

KeywordsTravel Writing and Literature; History, Culture, and Diplomacy; American History and Culture; Globalization; Sustainability; USable; Business history; Public relations; Political science; Political economy; Sociology; Business; Economics; Management; Law; Computer science; Ecology; World Wide Web; Biology

345. Globalisation and the evolution of international retailing: A comment on Alexander's ‘British overseas retailing, 1900–1960’

Business History 2012-04-20 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2012-04-20
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2011.631125
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Andrew GodleyUniversity of Reading; Henley Business School , University of Reading, Whiteknights, Reading , Berkshire , UK; Henley Business School, University of Reading, Whiteknights, Reading, Berkshire, UK
Haiming HangUniversity of Reading; Henley Business School , University of Reading, Whiteknights, Reading , Berkshire , UK; Henley Business School, University of Reading, Whiteknights, Reading, Berkshire, UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Nicholas Alexander's (2011. British overseas retailing, 1900–60: International firm characteristics, market selections and entry modes. Business History, 53, 530–556) survey of British overseas retailers from 1900 to 1960 provides pathbreaking new evidence of international retailing activity during the first globalisation boom. The article surveys this and other recent evidence, and confirms that international retailing was far more significant up to 1929 than previously thought. This activity was overwhelmingly undertaken by non-retailers, however, and hence by multinationals whose advantages in retailing were fundamentally unsustainable over the long run. Even the department store format, the principal retail innovation of the period, was not internationalised primarily by multinationals. Rather it was diffused via indigenous entrepreneurs, driven by a rapidly growing global demand for western style fashion and dress.

KeywordsMigration, Ethnicity, and Economy; Globalization; Business history; Political science; Economic history; International trade; Economy; Economics; Economic geography; Market economy

347. Microcredit in Palestine (1995–2008): A business history perspective

Business History 2012-06-01 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2012-06-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2011.638501
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Paolo Di MartinoBirmingham City University; South and City College Birmingham; University College Birmingham; University of Birmingham; Birmingham Business School, Birmingham, UK
Shaker SarsourUniversity of Siena; University of Siena, Siena, Italy
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This article analyses the development and functioning of the microcredit industry in Palestine in the period between its establishment as a partially autonomous political entity (1994) and the Israeli invasion of the Gaza Strip (2008). The article shows how, despite the increase in potential demand for microcredit due to the deterioration of the economic environment, the growth of the sector has been below expectation. One of the most important causes of this phenomenon has been the reluctance to lend resulting from the growing risk of late or no repayment of loans. Using original data from one microcredit institution (Arab Centre for Agricultural Development) and a quantitative approach, the article investigates the causes of this problem. Results show that the risk of late repayment was negatively correlated to the level of interest rate, to macroeconomic conditions, and to the age of the borrower while it was positively associated to the share of investment in the Gaza Strip, and to the size of loans.

KeywordsMicrofinance and Financial Inclusion; Islamic Finance and Banking Studies; FinTech, Crowdfunding, Digital Finance; Business history; Palestine; Perspective (graphical); Business; Political science; Economics; Management; History; Ancient history; Computer science; Artificial intelligence
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldstitle

348. Family character and international entrepreneurship: A historical comparison of Italian and Spanish ‘new multinationals’

Business History 2012-07-19 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2012-07-19
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2012.687536
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Andrea ColliBocconi University; Department of Policy Analysis and Public Management , Bocconi University , Milan , Italy
Esteban García CanalUniversidad de Oviedo; Departamento de Administración de Empresas , University of Oviedo , Oviedo , Spain
Mauro F. GuillénDepartment of Management , Wharton University , Philadelphia , PA , USA
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Although family firms are traditionally associated with low levels of internationalisation, this paper shows that family ownership can generate opportunities for international entrepreneurship related to the exploitation abroad of the expertise and social capital developed at home. Specifically, it argues that family character favours international expansion in at least three ways: (1) by granting more freedom to the managers of the company to develop their business model; (2) by facilitating the transfer to, and exploitation of, this model in foreign markets; and (3) by making the adoption of governance structures based upon trust easier. Drawing on a comparison between the business history of selected Spanish and Italian ‘new multinationals’, support is found for these hypotheses.

KeywordsFamily Business Performance and Succession; Corporate Finance and Governance; Migration, Ethnicity, and Economy; Internationalization; Entrepreneurship; Business history; Character (mathematics); Corporate governance; Family business; International business; Business; Social capital; Economics; Market economy; Economic geography; Industrial organization; Marketing; International trade; Sociology; Management; Social science; Finance; Mathematics; Geometry
