718. Dis(abling) conceptions of disability sport: An analysis of institutional change in the Paralympic movement through the lens of boundary and practice work

Business History 2024-01-08 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2024-01-08
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2023.2297788
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Gérard SimonBrussels Management School; ICHEC Brussels Management School; ICHEC Brussels Management School, Brussels, Belgium
Ian BrittainCoventry University; Centre for Business in Society, Coventry University; Centre for Business in Society, Coventry University, Coventry, UK
Glauco De VitaCoventry University; Centre for Business in Society, Coventry University; Centre for Business in Society, Coventry University, Coventry, UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Using unique archival evidence on the evolution of the Paralympic movement from the mid-1940s to 1989, this paper advances our understanding of how new organisational forms emerge. Our historical narrative identifies a shift from the Games born out of a medically based and rehabilitative cultural ethos to a sport-performance centred, corporate model embodied in the establishment of the International Paralympic Committee (IPC) in 1989. We show how the interactions between boundary and practice work enabled and, at times, ‘dis-abled’ the creation of the IPC. Heeding calls to refocus on how institutional change affects, or is affected by, power and inequality, we contribute to business history mainly by unveiling the collective struggle that led to the demise of a normative system of disability sport built upon the legitimacy of medical values and knowledge, and how evolving norms underpinning the way disabled people are viewed within society have influenced this process of change.

KeywordsSport and Mega-Event Impacts; Management and Organizational Studies; Sports, Gender, and Society; Ethos; Legitimacy; Normative; Sociology; Underpinning; Elite; Narrative; Embodied cognition; Power (physics); Work (physics); Public relations; Political science; Law; Epistemology; Physics; Politics; Linguistics; Engineering; Quantum mechanics; Mechanical engineering; Civil engineering; Philosophy
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

719. Industrial path creation, a business case approach: Daniel Alonso Group from steelmaking to wind power

Business History 2024-01-30 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2024-01-30
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2024.2304791
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Guillermo AntuñaBarcelona School of Economics; Universitat de Barcelona; Department of Economic History, Institutions, Politics and World Economy, University of Barcelona
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

The need for European reindustrialisation has boosted the debate on regional path creation. Through a business case approach, this work contributes to this literature by exploring the history of Daniel Alonso Group, a paradigmatic example of an entire sectorial trajectory. Born in the steelmaking region of Asturias (Spain) in 1957 as a small metalworking workshop, DAG is nowadays represented worldwide by Windar Renovables, a top firm in the wind power sector. DAG reflects the 3-stage cumulative process that allowed the Asturian metalworking sector to survive industrial restructuring and create new development paths: (1) birth around the steelmaking industry, (2) endogenous growth based on self-reinforcing dynamics (3) delocking, diversification, and international expansion moving towards Industry 4.0.

KeywordsRegional Development and Policy; Regional resilience and development; Innovation Policy and R&D; Steelmaking; Group (periodic table); Path (computing); Power (physics); Business history; Corporate group; Business; Operations management; Manufacturing engineering; Industrial organization; Economics; Industrial engineering; Engineering; Management; Computer science; Metallurgy; Materials science; Physics; Corporate governance; Programming language; Quantum mechanics
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldskeywords

720. Why Do Some Conservative CEOs Publicly Support Liberal Causes? Organizational Ideology, Managerial Discretion, and CEO Sociopolitical Activism

Organization Science 2024-01-30 Available
JournalOrganization Science
Publication date2024-01-30
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1287/orsc.2022.17160
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Adam J. WowakUniversity of Notre Dame; Department of Management & Organization, Mendoza College of Business, University of Notre Dame, Notre Dame, Indiana 46556
John R. BusenbarkUniversity of Notre Dame; Department of Management & Organization, Mendoza College of Business, University of Notre Dame, Notre Dame, Indiana 46556
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

The phenomenon of chief executive officers (CEOs) speaking out on contested social issues is a recent one, as CEO sociopolitical activism was seen as overly risky, and even taboo, for most of American business history. Yet, CEOs are increasingly choosing sides in societal debates despite the inherent risk of alienating stakeholders who disagree with the CEO’s stance. Even more puzzlingly, conservative CEOs sometimes espouse liberal stances in such debates, which runs counter to the otherwise consistent evidence in the upper echelons literature that CEOs are guided by their own values in their actions. Our study addresses this paradox by examining the antecedents of CEO liberal activism, with an emphasis on the interplay between the CEO’s ideology and the prevailing ideological tilt of the employee population in driving CEOs’ activism decisions. Drawing on the concept of managerial discretion, or latitude of action, we theorize that a pronounced organizational ideology constrains a CEO’s ability to act in accordance with their own values—particularly in the highly symbolic domain of activism. We also argue that conservative CEOs are relatively more focused on instrumental rationales for activism, whereas liberal CEOs tend to prioritize more intrinsic rationales, a consequence of which is that conservative CEOs more heavily weight organizational ideology in their activism decisions. We test our theory in the context of a highly publicized letter signed by nearly 100 CEOs of public companies in opposition to North Carolina’s controversial 2016 “bathroom bill.” Relying on a variety of novel data sources, we find robust support for our theory. Supplemental Material: The online appendix is available at https://doi.org/10.1287/orsc.2022.17160 .

KeywordsCorporate Finance and Governance; Political Influence and Corporate Strategies; Labor Movements and Unions; Discretion; Ideology; Business; Public relations; Accounting; Political economy; Politics; Law and economics; Political science; Economics; Law
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

721. <b>Marx in the Field</b> (Anthem Frontiers of Global Political Economy and Development) Edited by AlessandraMezzadri, London: Anthem Press. 2021. 256 pp., ISBN: 9781785274497, $125.00, h/b.

British Journal of Industrial Relations 2024-02-16 Available
JournalBritish Journal of Industrial Relations
Publication date2024-02-16
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/bjir.12792
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Kendra BrikenUniversity of Strathclyde; University of Strathclyde Glasgow UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Critical realism has become one of the go-to research philosophies in critical studies of work and employment, and specifically empirical, qualitative approaches. This metatheory appeals especially to those utilizing the case-study approach, a long-validated staple in the Western labour studies traditions. In this context, the ‘real’, understood as ‘structures with generative powers’, is supposed to be predefined by a scientifically approved theoretical approach to be chosen by the researcher. What if the choice would be historical materialism, and hence the said ‘real’ would be the sum total of the relations of production? The ‘real foundations’ in this Marxist understanding produce and frame forms of consciousness, and, arguably, actions, or, agency, develop. The contributions to the edited volume ‘Marx in the Field’ start with and differ from many studies inspired by critical realism in that they are based on a standpoint epistemology, hence giving the notion of criticality a different notion. The assumption is that engaging with people exposed to inequality and injustices gives insights to knowledge that is dismissed or invisible in normal sciences. The analytical in this perspective emerges from concrete actual and historical observations, and by necessity has to look for alternative ways to generate findings. Marxist categories are both causal and relational, and the connection between analytical framing and empirical approach are complicated. The standpoint is class in the broadest sense. But empirically, the tension of the contradictions of the world as it is and appears under capitalism. In the process of reification, social facts appear as naturalized ‘givens’. Through the lens of a Marxist approach, as shown in the book under review, these givens need to be questioned both at the level of categories and data. Every element is just as much a whole as it is an individual phenomenon or, experience. Totality here means to understand the contradictions of social being, both for and from the standpoint of the oppressed. This partiality is often criticized as functionalist, structuralist, deterministic, or, positivist research. In part, this is due to the fact that the methodological quibbles that come with Marxist lead research are rarely reflected on in an accessible and interdisciplinary manner. ‘Marx in the Field’ aims to fill this gap. The editor, Alessandra Mezzadri, hopes the book will be a ‘practical guide on how to carry out concrete, meaningful Marxian analysis in specific contemporary settings’ (p. 2). In her cautious and insightful introduction, Mezzadri combines her own experiences, traces inspirations and aspirations and sets the scene: the edition's Marxian take on the political economy means to ‘study the development of capitalist forces globally’ (p. 9). The assumption is that the dialectics of modernity and progress in a deeply interconnected global present challenges geographical, disciplinary and methodological boundaries. Bernstein (Chapter 2) systematically reflects on methodological consequences and choices that come with the core question in Marxist perspective: what is visible and how then should it be accessed to become empirically researchable? The following contributions are unified by addressing this question, and in their dedication to support fellow researchers by outlining the challenges the authors experienced with, ‘learning Marx while doing’ as Selwyn puts it (Chapter 8). This unifying aspect is mirroring feminist traditions to outline challenges and to include the reader into the debate rather than writing in an authoritative voice. By the same token, the contributions are presenting issues and riddles that are core to a Marxist perspective, that is, a critical approach towards qualitative and quantitative methods. Reflections on the reliability of institutional data is one obvious point, but how to solve the issue? In the tradition of critical theory, quantitative methods have the capacity to go beyond positivist assumptions. An intriguing example is the design and use of surveys conducted in the South African context. Investigating class relations, Mtero, Bunce, Cousins, Dubb and Hornby (Chapter 10) use households as unit of analysis given they ‘combine a multiplicity of income sources, including local and migrant wage labour, self-employment (…) and welfare payment by the state’ (p. 129). The shape shifting of these indicators lead the authors to developing scales to capture ‘livelihood’ trajectories to identify overall pattern in relative wealth accumulation and households. Here, the notion of reproduction, or, ‘making life’ is pushed towards its opposites: death and sickness become important categories to understand the workings of capitalist production. Harris-Whyte in her contribution focussing on ‘Marx's Merchants’ Capital’ discussed her finding on the shape shifting of agrarian markets in contemporary India. While reflecting on the importance of language and translation, discussing textbook notions of sampling ‘success’ and what this would mean in her field, and how established archives for business history need to be questioned for their reliability, she delivers a very smart case supporting Marx's idea that ‘[A]ll science would be superfluous if the outward appearance and the essence of things would directly coincided’. These chapters are exemplary, and showcase how in light of major shifts in employment relations, Westernized research might benefit from the potential for methodological innovation as well as rigorous critique of ‘scientific’ methodologies that are inspired by Marxian political economy approaches. To pay tribute to the inclusive and collegiate voices of this inspiring collection, I would like to telescopically highlight the scale and scope of content and concepts addressed: Mezzadri and Selwyn engage with sectoral studies and the interference of reproductive exploitation in the labour process; Newman, Hanies and Harris-White guide through Vol II inspired merchant capital projects; Toffanin (health), Stevano (poor's nourishment) and Vertommen (surrogacy industry) put the body to work; Sinha concludes with ‘Postcolonial Marxism and the Cyber Field in COVID times’ (Chapter 16). ‘Marx in the Field’ contributes to the analysis of concrete situations, and to an understanding of fields of inquiry that are intersectioned with actions governed by interest. Just as ‘class’ is not monolithic, neither is ‘capital’, but empirically they both consist of specific social groups with often shared agency based on heterogenous materiality (see Jan in their contribution ‘The ties that divide’, Chapter 4). The book is an invitation for researchers who have decided on their approach and are seeking for collegiate support. As Harris-White writes, ‘the story will be more complete than the evidence for it’ (p. 41), and the idea is to critically reflect on ‘Marx in the Field’, not to engage in metatheoretical debates. One important lesson to be learnt is that to discover the ‘forces behind the leitmotif of accumulation’ (p. 150) as Lombardazzi remarks in her critical reflection on notions of free and unfree labour based on the case of cotton industry in Uzbekistan (Chapter 11), engaging with Marx in the field is taking a very material toll, a fact that is unfortunately less discussed in the book. Acting ‘with Marx’ in the field is based on longue durée and specific sensitivities and resources. Researchers’ own situatedness is crucial to deliver the powerful critical insights the book provides into the workings of capitalism and collective struggles in global perspective. To paraphrase Marx, just as every researcher must wrestle with the field to satisfy their wants, to maintain and reproduce life, so must the Marxian researcher, and they must do so in all social formations, and under all possible modes of production. No shortcuts.

KeywordsPolitical Economy and Marxism; Anthem; Politics; Political science; Field (mathematics); Political economy; Economic history; Sociology; Economics; History; Art history; Law; Mathematics; Pure mathematics
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

722. Running on coffee: Paradox persistence in the US coffee industry, 1910–2020

Business History 2024-02-19 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2024-02-19
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2024.2310513
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Andrea TunarosaUniversity of Tampa; The University of Tampa
Patrick LêNEOMA Business School
Camille PradiesEcole des Hautes Etudes Commerciales du Nord; EDHEC Business School
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Organisations, industries, and societies often grapple with paradoxes – opposing and interrelated demands that persist over time. While questions of opposition and interrelation have received extensive scholarly attention, we know little about persistence, or why paradoxes resist closure and repeatedly resurface, regardless of how they are managed. Drawing on a qualitative historical analysis of the US coffee industry from the 1910s to the 2020s, we explore the temporal intricacies of paradox persistence through the ongoing tension between the industry’s idealism and pragmatism. We identify three mechanisms – ­catalysing contextual events, pole sedimentation, and ongoing attempts at balancing – that intertwine paradoxical concerns with history and characterise paradox persistence as both continuity and change over time. Our study contributes to paradox theory and business history by emphasising the significance of history and meaning evolution for paradox persistence, as well as the elusive nature of balancing processes.

KeywordsGlobal trade, sustainability, and social impact; Wine Industry and Tourism; Persistence (discontinuity); Economics; Engineering; Geotechnical engineering
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

723. Introducing the inverted Icarus paradox in business history – Evidence from David and Goliath in the Swedish telecommunications industry 1981–1990

Business History 2024-03-05 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2024-03-05
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2023.2292134
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Klas ErikssonStockholm School of Economics; Center for Statecraft and Strategic Communication, Stockholm School of Economics; Center for Statecraft and Strategic Communication, Stockholm School of Economics, Stockholm, Sweden
Erik LakomaaStockholm School of Economics; Institute for Economic and Business History Research, Stockholm School of Economics; Institute for Economic and Business History Research, Stockholm School of Economics, Stockholm, Sweden
Rasmus NykvistStockholm School of Economics; Institute for Economic and Business History Research, Stockholm School of Economics; Institute for Economic and Business History Research, Stockholm School of Economics, Stockholm, Sweden
Christian SandströmRatio; Jönköping University; Jönköping International Business School & the Ratio Institute, Jönköping International Business School, Jönköping University; Jönköping International Business School &amp; the Ratio Institute, Jönköping International Business School, Jönköping University, Jönköping, Sweden
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Previous research in business and management history has identified the Icarus paradox, which describes how organisations may fall due to overconfidence and hubris. We build upon previous research on paradoxes in business history and introduce the notion of an inverted Icarus paradox. Using rich archival sources coded in a relational database, we show how an entrant firm, Comvik, outmanoeuvred an established government monopoly in the non-market domain from 1980 to 1990, despite inferior resources and a weak market position. The government monopoly Televerket faced an inverted Icarus paradox; it could not leverage its strengths and political connections as they were stuck in a David versus Goliath narrative where public opinion was more sympathetic to the entrant firm Comvik.

KeywordsInnovation and Knowledge Management; Innovation Diffusion and Forecasting; Economic Theory and Institutions; ICARUS; Telecommunications; Business history; Telecommunications equipment; Economics; Business; Engineering; Management; Particle physics; Physics
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldstitle

724. The strategic realignment of paradoxical family and business goals in family business: A rhetorical history perspective

Business History 2024-03-05 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2024-03-05
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2024.2317938
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Lara PecisCardiff University; Cardiff Business School, Cardiff University; Cardiff Business School, Cardiff University, Cardiff, UK
Bingbing GeLancaster University; Entrepreneurship and Strategy, Lancaster University Management School; Entrepreneurship and Strategy, Lancaster University Management School, Lancaster, UK
Florian BauerUniversity of Bristol; University of Bristol Business School; University of Bristol Business School, Bristol, UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Family firms are paradoxical by nature due to the interplay of two distinct goal systems: the family and the firm. These systems involve nested tensions that can create apparent paradoxes over time. Taking a rhetorical history lens, we explore how family firms can dynamically produce temporal equilibria between goal systems through the strategic use of history. Empirically, we investigate the emergence and development of two apparent paradoxes unfolding through the history of the growth of Alpha, an Italian family firm in the packaging industry. Our findings suggest that rhetorical history can alleviate the tensions emergent from the paradoxical goal systems of family businesses. Our research provides a unique contribution by revealing the emergence and agentic process of the co-construction of rhetorical history, which involves multiple agencies from both family and non-family employees. Moreover, such co-created rhetorical history can dynamically produce temporal equilibria in family business’s persistent paradoxical goal systems.

KeywordsFamily Business Performance and Succession; Entrepreneurship Studies and Influences; Business history; Rhetorical question; Perspective (graphical); Family business; Business; Public relations; Political science; Management; Marketing; Economics; Philosophy; Linguistics; Artificial intelligence; Computer science
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldskeywords

725. Revise and resubmit? Peer reviewing business historical research

Business History 2024-03-14 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2024-03-14
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2024.2325610
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Christina LubinskiCopenhagen Business School; Department of Business Humanities and Law, Copenhagen Business School, Frederiksberg, Denmark
Stephanie DeckerUniversity of Birmingham; Department of Strategy and International Business, University of Birmingham, Birmingham, UK
Niall MacKenzieAdam Smith Institute; University of Glasgow; Adam Smith Business School, University of Glasgow, Glasgow, UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Introduction. Peer review in Business History has benefitted from the insights of over 550 expert reviewers over the past three years. In this editorial, we contextualise the journal’s peer review process historically and in the available literature. We discuss some of the challenges of the double-blind review system, including securing reviewer engagement amidst time constraints and publication pressure, the dark sides of anonymisation, and the role of bias, values, and interdisciplinarity in research evaluations. Based on these reflections, we furnish some concrete guidance for reviewers on how to approach business historical research. We end by contemplating the future landscape of peer review and opening a conversation about review standards and reviewer education in the field of business history. Is peer review ripe for revision and resubmit?

KeywordsManagement and Organizational Studies; Management Theory and Practice; Business history; Business; Public relations; Accounting; Marketing; Political science; Management; Economics
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

726. Green entrepreneurship in UK foods and the emergence of the alternative meat sector: Quorn 1965–2006

Business History 2024-05-26 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2024-05-26
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2024.2354816
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Andrew GodleyUniversity of Sussex; University of Sussex Business School, University of Sussex; University of Sussex Business School, University of Sussex, Brighton, UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

&lt;p dir="ltr"&gt;The pioneer in alternative proteins was the British company Marlow Foods, which created the world’s first mycoprotein product under the Quorn brand name. This article explores its emergence and development between 1965 and 2006. This case study supports three of the broad premises in the existing literature on business history of green entrepreneurship; that green innovation typically takes longer than non-green, is therefore more costly, and that green entrepreneurs are typically values- not profit-driven. The article then explores these premises and their consequences in the case of Quorn. With the long period between its inception and market acceptance in the 1990s, there was a significant continuity gap in Quorn’s environmental credentials. Quorn bridged that gap because of a sustained commitment to environmentalism that came &lt;i&gt;via&lt;/i&gt; the legacy of its very influential founder. But it was the BSE crisis that permitted the product to move from vegetarian niche to the mainstream.&lt;/p&gt;

KeywordsAgriculture Sustainability and Environmental Impact; Organic Food and Agriculture; Food Waste Reduction and Sustainability; Entrepreneurship; Business; Economics; Agricultural economics; Food science; Biology; Finance
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

727. Josiah Wedgwood, business history, and our modes of enquiry

Business History 2024-06-07 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2024-06-07
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2024.2359724
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Andrew PoppCopenhagen Business School; Copenhagen Business School, Copenhagen, Denmark
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Taking its departure in the correspondence of eighteenth-century entrepreneur Josiah Wedgwood, focusing on his development and use of two field-based metaphors or heuristics, this article explores two modes of enquiry employed in business history and argues for the maintenance and strengthening of ties to humanistic modes of historical enquiry. In doing so, the essay identifies a loose genre of agenda-setting books and articles within business history, to which it seeks to add a modest proposal.

KeywordsHistorical Economic and Social Studies; Philosophy, History, and Historiography; American History and Culture; Business history; Heuristics; Humanism; Field (mathematics); Sociology; Business practice; Management; Epistemology; Political science; Philosophy; Law; Economics; Business; Computer science; Business administration; Operating system; Pure mathematics; Mathematics

728. Taking stock and moving forward: What makes a contribution in business history?

Business History 2024-06-20 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2024-06-20
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2024.2362360
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Stephanie DeckerUniversity of Birmingham; Birmingham Business School, University of Birmingham, Birmingham, UK
Christina LubinskiCopenhagen Business School; Department of Business Humanities, Copenhagen Business School, Frederiksberg, Denmark
Niall MacKenzieAdam Smith Institute; University of Glasgow; Adam Smith Business School, University of Glasgow, Glasgow, UK
Nic FeltonING Direct; Managing Editor for Business History
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This editorial reflects on the research published in Business History in the last few years in the context of wider discussions in the field of business history. In particular, we consider the authors and their thematic orientation, reflection on the geographic and disciplinary distribution and gender balance in the field. Articles published cluster around specific time periods, countries and regions, with early modern or medieval history, and research beyond Europe and North America less well represented. On the basis of this survey, we consider the journal’s recently changed aims and scope and what we see as a contribution to knowledge in business history. We particularly consider the role of theory in historical research and affirm our commitment to a range of relevant contributions in the journal. These are empirical, historiographical and theoretical in nature, and support our strategy to maintain Business History as a journal publishing interdisciplinary and diverse research.

KeywordsHistorical Economic and Social Studies; Management and Organizational Studies; Social and Cultural Dynamics; Business history; Stock (firearms); Financial economics; Economics; Business; History; Economic history; Archaeology

Matched fieldstitle

730. Agents, brokerage and Argentinian railways 1880–1905

Business History 2024-10-23 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2024-10-23
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2024.2410871
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Tehreem HusainLondon School of Economics and Political Science; Department of Economic History, London School of Economics; Department of Economic History, London School of Economics, London, UK
Emily BuchneaNorthumbria University; Newcastle Business School, Northumbria University; Newcastle Business School, Northumbria University, Newcastle, UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This article examines the role of agents and how their brokerage activities influenced the composition, shape and size of Baring’s business networks in Argentinian railways during 1880–1905. Baring was heavily involved in Argentine sovereign and railway financing and relied on trusted agents, who acted as important conduits of information, to manage their business empire. We explore 2700 pieces of correspondence in the form of letters and telegrams to illustrate Baring’s network over time. Studying the case of Argentinian railways, the correspondences reveal the longevity of Baring’s network and agents’ brokerage roles over time in response to changing local and global economic currents. Through this rich qualitative data and network analysis, we contribute to understanding agent’s brokerage and network change over time. By highlighting the role of invisible actors in the form of agents, the article contributes to understanding infrastructure finance, financial globalisation and more generally, the global history of capitalism.

KeywordsArgentine historical studies; History and Politics in Latin America; Global trade, sustainability, and social impact; Business; Commerce; Industrial organization; Business history; Economics; Management
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldskeywords

731. Business history and the resilience of regional economies

Business History 2024-11-16 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2024-11-16
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2024.2432420
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Ramón Ramón-MuñozBarcelona School of Economics; Universitat de Barcelona; Department of Economic History, Institutions, Politics and World Economy, University of Barcelona
Marijn MolemaUniversity of Groningen; Department of Economic and Social History, Rijksuniversiteit Groningen
Emil EvenhuisNetherlands Environmental Assessment Agency; PBL - Netherlands Environmental Assessment Agency, The Hague
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

While resilience has become a key concern within a variety of disciplines in recent years, it requires further development in the field of business history. With the aim of providing new conceptual and empirical insights into the study of regional business history, in this introduction to this special issue we provide a general overview to the literature on regional economic resilience and to the concept of resilience. This is followed by a review of the recent research on business history that use the resilience concept. We then establish the analytical framework and present the aims and boundaries of our research. In the next section, we briefly describe the content of the articles included in this special issue. We finally discuss these articles in order to shed light on the uniqueness and universality applied to the resilience of regional economies.

KeywordsRegional resilience and development; Employment and Welfare Studies; Migration, Aging, and Tourism Studies; Business history; Resilience (materials science); Business; Economics; Economy; Economic history; Physics; Thermodynamics

733. Lightning Strikes Back: Lightning Fire, Standard Oil, and Anti-monopoly in the Pennsylvanian Oil Fields, 1859–1897

The Business History Review 2025-01-01 Available
JournalThe Business History Review
Publication date2025-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1017/s0007680526101391
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Minseok JangUniversity at Albany, State University of New York; History, University at Albany
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract This article examines how lightning fires shaped anti-monopoly sentiment among Pennsylvanian oilmen in the late nineteenth century. Drawing on 138 lightning-fire incidents coded from local periodicals, the study investigates the environmental impacts of Standard Oil’s expansion in the Pennsylvania oil fields—particularly how its oil storage infrastructure attracted lightning and thus increased the risk of oil fires. Leveraging its monopsonistic position, Standard Oil sought to financialize this environmental risk and shift it onto independent producers, inventing a quasi-fire-insurance system called the “general average assessment.” Viewing this practice as a major threat to their business, oilmen developed bottom-up antagonism toward Standard Oil. Ultimately, this study offers a new framework for integrating environmental and business history by showing that the financialization of environmental risk acts as a central arena where corporate power is consolidated, contested, and politically reconfigured.

KeywordsAmerican History and Culture; American Environmental and Regional History; Global Energy and Sustainability Research; Lightning (connector); Lightning strike; Pennsylvanian; Meteorology; Petroleum industry; Power (physics); Geology; Forensic engineering; Receivership; Financialization; Environmental science; Petroleum; Vulnerability (computing); Engineering; Electricity
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

734. Remembering Shakila Yacob (1962–2025)

The Business History Review 2025-01-01 Available
JournalThe Business History Review
Publication date2025-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1017/s0007680526101615
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Teresa da Silva LopesUniversity of York
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

The sudden passing of Professor Shakila Yacob is a loss to the international community of business historians and public policy specialists.Over a career spanning four decades, she helped place Malaysia firmly on the global map of business history scholarship while building intellectual bridges between historians, management scholars, economists, political scientists, and practitioners of public policy.Her work combined archival depth with a strong sensitivity to the political and moral dimensions of enterprise in post-colonial societies.She was a global scholar-curious, generous, and committed to ensuring that voices from Southeast Asia were heard in international scholarly debates.Shakila's academic formation was itself transnational, reflecting the breadth that would later define her scholarship.She began her studies at the University of Malaya, where she completed a Bachelor of Arts in Southeast Asian Studies in 1985.At a time when Malaysian historiography was still largely dominated by political and social history, she was drawn to the intersections between history, politics, and business, an interest that would shape her later engagement with international business history.Her intellectual horizons expanded further when she pursued a master's degree in political science at Western Michigan University, where she graduated with distinction and was awarded the Lawrence Ziring Award for academic excellence.Exposure to American political science, comparative politics, and international relations sharpened her analytical tools and fostered a lasting interest in United States-Asia relations, which later informed her work on trade, foreign direct investment, and lobbying.Shakila's doctoral studies at the University of Reading, under the supervision of Geoffrey Jones, marked a decisive turning point in her scholarly trajectory.She was the last doctoral student he supervised.Jones's work on multinational enterprises, entrepreneurship, and corporate responsibility offered her a new model of scholarship that combined historical depth with relevance to contemporary debates.She was also exposed to a tradition of business history that combined it with economic theory, particularly international business theory, and treated firms as political and social actors, not merely economic agents.

KeywordsJewish and Middle Eastern Studies; Interdisciplinary Cultural and Social Studies; Sociopolitical Dynamics in Nepal; Scholarship; Historiography; Politics; International relations; Sociology; The arts; Bachelor; Political science; Foreign policy; Media studies; Business history; International studies; Social science; Global politics; Comparative politics; Political history; Work (physics); Gender studies; Public policy; Public relations; African studies; Immigration; Cultural studies; Intellectual history; International business; Political management; American studies; Management; Liberal arts education; Foreign relations; History; Discipline
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

735. Translating Emergent Technologies into Novel Therapeutics: Tracing Complementarity and Co-evolution in the Cambridge–Boston Innovation Ecosystem

The Business History Review 2025-01-01 Available
JournalThe Business History Review
Publication date2025-01-01
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1017/s000768052510086x
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Maki UmemuraCardiff University; Cardiff University, UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract This article traces the history of the life sciences business in the Cambridge–Boston area and explores how it became the global epicenter of the modern therapeutics industry. While business history scholarship on therapeutics is extensive, few have studied recent technological modalities—from therapeutic proteins to cell and gene therapies—or adopted a regional ecosystem perspective. Based on archival materials and oral histories, this research bridges these works and incorporates insights from the innovation ecosystems framework. It considers how dynamic interactions between an evolving network of complementary and interdependent actors, including therapeutics firms, universities, hospitals, and risk capital providers, enhanced innovative capacity. This perspective also illuminates how ecosystem strength derived from the co-evolution of actors—from universities restructuring technology transfer offices to academic scientists becoming entrepreneurs. The research further highlights the nonlinearity of innovation processes. It shows how an extraordinary interplay between structural advantage, serendipitous timing, and strategic actions cultivated an unparalleled capacity to translate emergent technologies into novel therapies.

KeywordsUniversity-Industry-Government Innovation Models; Biomedical and Engineering Education; Neuroethics, Human Enhancement, Biomedical Innovations; Complementarity (molecular biology); Restructuring; Interdependence; Business ecosystem; Scholarship; Knowledge management; Business; Emerging technologies; Corporate governance; Innovation management; Perspective (graphical); Ecosystem; Crowds; Data science; Human capital; Management science; Structural holes; Tracing; Business model; Centrality; Sociology; Computer science; Technological change; Network theory; Strategic management; Open innovation; Capital (architecture)
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

736. The Business School and the End of History: Reimagining Management Education

Academy of Management Learning and Education 2025-01-13 Available
JournalAcademy of Management Learning and Education
Publication date2025-01-13
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.5465/amle.2024.0033
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Ken StarkeyUniversity of Nottingham
Sue TempestUniversity of Nottingham
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

We contend that we underestimate the importance of understanding the history of ideas for pluralism and reflexivity in management education. We explain this failing through the lens of “the end of history” argument (Fukuyama, 1989) which suggests that there are no viable alternatives to current assumptions about business and society, making business schools too neoliberal in their core ideas and stuck in a time warp by this worldview. We contribute theoretically to debates about the role of history in helping us to better understand the creation of our ideas and dominant logics. In our disciplined provocation, we highlight the valuable role management education could play in raising historical consciousness for enhanced social imagination. In our call to action, we highlight how examining the history of our ideas combined with the humanities offers the possibility of a more nuanced and reflective approach to management education. We argue that a pluralism of social imaginaries and world-making would generate new debates and a deeper sense of management practice as a humanistic enterprise. This, we argue, is vital to developing more inclusive, hopeful, and humane social imaginaries for management learning that is more responsive to a context of complex business and social challenges.

KeywordsManagement and Organizational Studies; Management Theory and Practice; Management and Marketing Education; Business history; Business education; End of history; Management; Sociology; Business management; Pedagogy; Business; Political science; Higher education; Business administration; Economics; Law; Politics
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldskeywords

737. Organising through time: Paradox and history

Business History 2025-02-12 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2025-02-12
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2025.2461064
Author & affiliation

Stewart CleggThe University of Sydney; School of Project Management, University of Sydney
Miguel Piña e CunhaUniversidade Nova de Lisboa; Nova School of Business and Economics, Universidade NOVA de Lisboa
Charles HarveyNewcastle University; Newcastle University Business School
Mairi MacleanUniversity of Bath; School of Management, University of Bath
Álvaro Ferreira da SilvaUniversidade Nova de Lisboa; Nova School of Business and Economics, Universidade NOVA de Lisboa
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This special issue represents the first effort to bridge paradox theory and historical analysis further. Its goal is to create a fertile environment for advancing the historical study of organisations by contributing to discussions on various research topics where organisations, paradoxes, and temporality intersect. Defining organisational paradoxes as interdependent contradictions that persist over time contains an immediate plea for applying historical thinking and methodologies. The collection of papers included in this special issue illustrates that exploring paradoxes in business history is a promising research avenue.

739. From globalisation to autarky. business strategies, governmental policies and international interests in the Italian aluminium industry (1928–1943)

Business History 2025-06-25 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2025-06-25
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2025.2517093
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Marco BertilorenziUniversity of Padua; Dipartimento di Scienze Storiche, Geografiche e dell’Antichità, Dissgea, Università degli Studi di Padova
Mario PeruginiUniversity of Catania; Dipartimento di Economia e Impresa, Università di Catania
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This article reviews the history of the Italian aluminium industry between 1928 and 1943. The development of national production of aluminium is a crucial episode in the industrial history of Italy and offers a rich insight into a variety of debates, most notably the nature and impact of autarky. This article shows how different policy regimes in 1920s and 1930s led foreign multinationals and national firms to change their strategic behaviour. One suggestion of this article is that the received narrative has overestimated the impact of the ideological and political meddling by the Fascist regime in the autarky economy, downplaying the pragmatic approach that characterised the big business strategies. One important conclusion here is also that, as with import-substitution policies in emerging economies in the post-World War II era, autarky offered a shelter behind which big Italian enterprises could fast develop a large domestic market, fundamental prerequisite for greater scale economies and technological catch-up.

KeywordsItaly: Economic History and Contemporary Issues; Italian Fascism and Post-war Society; Autarky; Globalization; Business history; Business; International trade; Economics; Market economy; Management; Welfare
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldskeywords

740. Modern Chinese enterprise and the global economy: A historiographical essay

Business History 2025-07-14 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2025-07-14
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2025.2525148
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Ghassan MoazzinUniversity of Hong Kong; HKIHSS & School of Humanities, The University of Hong Kong; HKIHSS & School of Humanities, The University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong, HKSAR
John D. WongUniversity of Modern Sciences; University of Hong Kong; HKIHSS & School of Modern Languages and Cultures, The University of Hong Kong; HKIHSS & School of Modern Languages and Cultures, The University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong, HKSAR
Jin-A KangHanyang University; Department of History, College of Humanities, Hanyang University; Department of History, College of Humanities, Hanyang University, Seoul, Republic of Korea
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

In this introduction to the special issue ‘The Global Economy and the Origins of Modern Chinese Business’, we first provide a historiographical overview of the development of modern Chinese business from the early nineteenth century to the 1970s, with a special focus on the transnational linkages of Chinese enterprises. We highlight how Chinese businesses engaged and interacted with the global economy and globalisation over one and a half centuries. We then introduce the six contributions to this special issue. These contributions cover case studies ranging from Shanxi remittance firms to Mao-era economic relations between China and Latin America, showcasing how Chinese businesses navigated and interacted with changing international dynamics. Next, the article explains common themes amongst the contributions and their larger implications for understanding the historical trajectory of Chinese business in relation to the global economy. Finally, the concluding discussion highlights several avenues for future research in Chinese business history.<br>

KeywordsHistorical Economic and Social Studies; Asian Industrial and Economic Development; China's Socioeconomic Reforms and Governance; Historiography; Business history; Economy; Economics; Political science; History; Economic history; Archaeology
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

741. Kneeling to violent men: Investors and insurrection in Imperial Russia

Business History 2025-08-12 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2025-08-12
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2025.2537690
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Christopher A. HartwellKozminski University; ZHAW Zurich University of Applied Sciences; International Business Policy, Zurich University of Applied Sciences (ZHAW) School of Management and Law; Management in Digital and Networked Societies, Kozminski University
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Where people face immature or contested political institutions – a characteristic of many lower-income countries – the lack of access to the ballot box may result in political violence. Terrorism, uprisings, and other forms of individual or collective action to shift power may result in damage, fatalities, and, above all, uncertainty about what is to happen next. How then do businesses respond to political violence? This paper argues that political violence can be an alternative way in which to understand business history in difficult environments, creating less of a ‘rupture’ in the history of a firm than a ‘structural break’. By using stock prices and popular press, researchers can triangulate the strategic responses of firms and sectors to political uncertainty. This methodological framework is developed using political violence in one country, Russia during the nineteenth century, to show the effect of such violence on firms and their environment.

KeywordsSoviet and Russian History; Kneeling; Economic history; Political science; Economics; Medicine; Pathology; Alternative medicine
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

742. From imposition to concession, from compliance to resistance: Creating a Harvard Business School clone in a Turkish university, 1954–1965

Business History 2025-08-28 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2025-08-28
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2025.2543956
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Behlül ÜsdikenSabancı Üniversitesi; Özyeğin University; Faculty of Business, Özyeğin University; Sabanci Business School, Sabanci University
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Drawing on archival sources, this study traces the early history of the 'Turkish Institute of Business Administration', established in 1954 within the Faculty of Economics at Istanbul University with Ford Foundation (FF) funding and Harvard Business School (HBS) guidance. The historical narrative describes what transpired on both the American and Turkish sides during this FF-HBS-initiated and directed transfer process. Through this account, the article contributes to the literature on post-World War II American influence on business education in three ways: First, it highlights how direct American interventions were shaped by who in the United States was involved, and when. Second, it demonstrates how tensions and power relations between the FF and HBS influenced both the direction and the outcome of the transfer. Finally, the process-based account shows how and why the reception of an imposed American model involved both full adoption and some significant deviations.

KeywordsHigher Education Governance and Development; Turkish; Compliance (psychology); Business history; clone (Java method); Resistance (ecology); Political science; Management; Business; Business administration; Economics; Psychology; DNA; Biology; Genetics; Social psychology; Linguistics; Philosophy; Ecology
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldskeywords

743. Methodological subversions in the absence of archives

Business History 2025-09-16 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2025-09-16
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2025.2542820
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Marta GasparinCopenhagen Business School; Department of Business Humanities and Law, Copenhagen Business School; Department of Business Humanities and Law, Copenhagen Business School, Copenhagen, Denmark
Martin QuinnLancaster University; Lancaster University Management School; Lancaster University Management School, Lancaster, United Kingdom
Steven D. BrownNottingham Trent University; Human Resource Management, Nottingham Business School, Nottingham Trent University; Human Resource Management, Nottingham Business School, Nottingham Trent University, Nottingham, United Kingdom
Enrico MacciòCopenhagen Business School; Department of Business Humanities and Law, Copenhagen Business School; Department of Business Humanities and Law, Copenhagen Business School, Copenhagen, Denmark
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This paper provides a methodological contribution around how the history of a sector can be researched when the records of the past have either not been maintained or erased. Set against the backdrop of Vietnam’s colonial history and experiences of political turmoil, we had to overcome this challenge creatively, understanding what made sense for that context, what could be asked, and how to collect data when it was not possible due to (auto)censorship or absence of archives. We developed two alternative methods of inquiry: mobilising objects to discuss stories that otherwise would be censored, which we refer to as an object-oriented method, and an art-based method, based on critical fabulations. We explain the challenges of using traditional business history methods in this context, present the process of developing these two novel methods through empirical accounts, and finally discuss the ethical implications and limitations researchers might face in their application.

KeywordsDigital and Traditional Archives Management; Data Analysis and Archiving; Oral History, Memory, Narrative Analysis; Business history; Face (sociological concept); Politics; Set (abstract data type); Process (computing); Colonialism; Sociology; Field (mathematics); Empirical evidence; History; Epistemology; Political science; Post colonialism; Empirical research; Historical record; Political history; Computer science; Data science; Positive economics; Political process
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

744. Monopolists for Competition? Incumbent Action by Telecom Operators and Financial Exchanges 1980–1990

Business History 2025-10-24 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2025-10-24
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2025.2539273
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Erik LakomaaStockholm School of Economics; Department of Marketing and Strategy, Stockholm School of Economics; Institute for Economic and Business History Research (EHFF), Stockholm School of Economics
Rasmus NykvistLinköping University; Stockholm School of Economics; Department of Management and Engineering, Linköping University; Institute for Economic and Business History Research (EHFF), Stockholm School of Economics
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Achieving and maintaining a monopolist position is often assumed to be among the objectives of a firm. However, there are instances where it is better to operate in a competitive market than remain a monopolist. Monopolistic firms may, therefore, push for deregulation - at least on their terms. We introduce the concept of monopolists for competition to analyse two such companies, Swedish telecom operator Televerket and the Stockholm Stock Exchange (SSE). Thus, the paper contributes to the literature on incumbent action and corporate political activity (CPA) by state-owned/state-controlled companies, a field that has received little interest in business history research. We find that the personal political skills of the companies' managers played a crucial role in the deregulatory process, as institutional obstacles prevented them from using many standard CPA tools. Fear of being perceived as monopolistic also restricted them from leveraging their market positions when controlling the evolving market structures.

KeywordsPolitical Influence and Corporate Strategies; Corporate Finance and Governance; Merger and Competition Analysis; Monopolistic competition; Deregulation; Industrial organization; Position (finance); Economics; Business; Competition (biology); Politics; Action (physics); Market economy; Stock (firearms); Stock market; Political action; Barriers to entry; Vertical integration; Microeconomics; Field (mathematics); Market structure; Financial services; Collective action; Operator (biology); Stock exchange; Level playing field; Financial market; Finance
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

745. Connecting the dots: Business history research on social entrepreneurship in the Global South

Business History 2025-11-19 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2025-11-19
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2025.2584546
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Léna ProuchetCopenhagen Business School; Business Humanities and Law, Copenhagen Business School; Business Humanities and Law, Copenhagen Business School, Copenhagen, Denmark
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Social Entrepreneurship (SE) in the Global South is often framed as a recent innovation introduced through Global North–driven development programmes. This perspective overlooks the long-standing and locally embedded practices of SE in these regions. Business history, emphasising contextual analysis and examining long-term change, offers essential tools for uncovering the diverse trajectories and meanings of SE. Yet, business historians have only recently started to engage with SE beyond the Global North. Through a narrative review, this article connects the dots between existing business history research on SE and work from adjacent social science disciplines such as entrepreneurship and development studies. It highlights both the strengths and limitations of current approaches and identifies underexplored areas, such as grassroots and Indigenous forms of SE. The article concludes by outlining future research directions that foreground local agency, offer alternative visions of social impact and expand the conceptual boundaries of SE within business history.

KeywordsEntrepreneurship Studies and Influences; Innovation and Socioeconomic Development; Migration, Ethnicity, and Economy; Vision; Business history; Social entrepreneurship; Grassroots; Entrepreneurship; Indigenous; Global South; Perspective (graphical); Sociology; Public relations; Work (physics); Social business; Political science; Narrative; Business model; Business studies; Small business; International business; New business development; Engineering ethics; Business Review; Economic growth; Social history (medicine); Business development; Social innovation; Social science; Conceptual framework; Regional science; Rubric; Political economy
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldstitle

746. How Do SMEs Respond to Deglobalization? Insights from Italian SMEs in the Interwar Period (1936–1943)

Journal of Management Studies 2025-11-19 Available
JournalJournal of Management Studies
Publication date2025-11-19
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/joms.70033
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Valeria GiacominBocconi University
Francesco RomagnoliBocconi University
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract This study investigates how small and medium‐sized enterprises (SMEs) respond to deglobalization and economic nationalism, using historical evidence from fascist Italy, a period of autarky and restricted international trade. While prior research has focused primarily on larger firms, especially multinational enterprises (MNEs), the strategic behaviour of the resource‐constrained category of SMEs remains underexplored in both management and business history literatures. Drawing on archival material from the Istituto Mobiliare Italiano and historical sources, we identify four adaptive strategies: (i) market repositioning, (ii) national re‐branding, (iii) disguising core business, and (iv) mobilizing formal ties. Our findings reveal that, while SMEs draw from the strategic playbook of MNEs, they implement these strategies in distinct ways due to internal and external constraints. In doing so, they exhibit both strategic and political ambidexterity to adapt. They leverage their distinctive flexibility and agility by mobilizing internal capabilities (e.g., brand identity), and resources (e.g., formal international ties). This enables SMEs’ market repositioning and allows them to achieve symbolic alignment with government agendas through reshaping narratives to secure critical resources. Ultimately, this leads to bolder strategies like pivoting from global niche to broader domestic markets. This framework offers insights into SMEs’ resilience under deglobalization and economic nationalism.

KeywordsItaly: Economic History and Contemporary Issues; International Business and FDI; Family Business Performance and Succession; Multinational corporation; Ambidexterity; Leverage (statistics); Business; Flexibility (engineering); Embeddedness; Politics; Narrative; Government (linguistics); Industrial organization; Psychological resilience; Competitive advantage; International business; Economic system; Dynamic capabilities; Resilience (materials science); Strategic management; Business history; Core (optical fiber); Economics; Market economy; Institutional theory; Scope (computer science); Resource (disambiguation); International trade; International market; Economy
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldsabstract

747. Trade Wars, International Supply Chains, and Business Associations: Historical Evidence from Norway and Portugal

Journal of Management Studies 2025-11-19 Available
JournalJournal of Management Studies
Publication date2025-11-19
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/joms.70026
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Rolv Petter AmdamBI Norwegian Business School
Teresa da Silva LopesUniversity of York
Trudi Henrydotter EikremVolda University College
María Eugénia MataUniversidade Nova de Lisboa
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract This article examines the 1921–1923 trade war between Norway and Portugal as a historical case for exploring business responses to deglobalization and trade disruption. Focusing on the international supply chains (ISCs) of port wine and salted and dried cod, it investigates how business actors contributed to the recoupling of ISCs after a trade war. Drawing on a history‐to‐theory methodology and extensive archival research, the study offers new theoretical insights into how institutional environments shape business agency during periods of deglobalization. It develops a typology of trade wars structured around two dimensions: the degree of coordination within ISCs and the economic reach of the conflict. The findings demonstrate that, in processes of symmetric recoupling , where both nations restore disrupted supply chains on equal terms, business associations as well as multinational enterprises (MNEs) play a pivotal role in business diplomacy and supply‐chain reconstruction. The symmetric nature of the post‐trade war recoupling fosters mutual recovery and enhances long‐term competitiveness for the industries involved. The typology proposed also provides a framework for analysing other historical and contemporary trade wars across diverse geopolitical settings.

KeywordsInternational Business and FDI; Global trade and economics; Management and Organizational Studies; Typology; Multinational corporation; Geopolitics; Supply chain; Diplomacy; Agency (philosophy); International trade; Business; International business; Business history; Port (circuit theory); Economics; Economy; Business model; Economic geography; Industrial organization; Commerce; Trade barrier; Supply and demand
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldskeywords

748. Where have all the business women gone? Women and business in the 20th century

Business History 2026-01-09 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2026-01-09
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2025.2602637
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Selin DilliUtrecht University; Department of History and Art History, Utrecht University
Carry van LieshoutThe Open University; Department of Geography, The Open University
Jennifer AstonNorthumbria University; Northumbria School of Law, Northumbria University
Robert J. BennettUniversity of Cambridge; Bridge University; The Cambridge Group for History of Population, University of Cambridge
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

This Special Issue (SI) explores the development of female entrepreneurship throughout the twentieth century. The articles employ a mixed-methods approach to showcase various forms of female entrepreneurship, from self-employment and co-working in family businesses to innovators across underrepresented sectors, economic conditions, and institutional contexts in both Western and non-Western settings. The SI illustrates how qualitative and quantitative methods work together to provide new insights into how women pursued business opportunities in non-Western environments characterised by different paths of industrialisation, regional diversity, and business structures. Focusing on the twentieth century—which has received less attention in the history of female entrepreneurship—the articles also engage with current debates on female entrepreneurship. We offer a systematic review of existing literature, highlighting key themes, methods, and findings from prior research, along with their limitations. We then emphasise the contributions of this Special Issue in filling these gaps and suggest directions for future research.

KeywordsFamily Business Performance and Succession; Entrepreneurship Studies and Influences; Migration, Ethnicity, and Economy; Entrepreneurship; Work (physics); Business history; Women entrepreneurs; Female entrepreneurs; Political science; Public relations; Sociology; Qualitative research; Economic growth; Key (lock); Small business; Qualitative property; Family business; Business Review; Business model; Business; Gender studies; Political economy
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldskeywords

749. Platonic Business Ethics: A Survey of the Past and a Discussion of the Future

Journal of Business Ethics 2026-02-06 Available
JournalJournal of Business Ethics
Publication date2026-02-06
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-026-06267-y
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Stephen DunneUniversity of Edinburgh; Business School, University of Edinburgh, Room 2.34, 29 Buccleuch Place, Edinburgh, EH8 9JS, Scotland
Robert CluleyUniversity of Birmingham; Birmingham Business School, University of Birmingham, Edgbaston, Birmingham, B15 2TT, UK
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract This paper discusses the achievements, the possibilities, and the challenges of Platonic business ethics. Through a systematic scoping review of 695 articles published in 13 business and management journals, it undertakes a knowledge-stock analysis of the almost complete range of Plato’s written texts that have influenced business ethicists and identifies five prominent ways in which they have applied his work. It finds that Plato’s Republic has had the most enduring influence throughout the history of the discipline and details how Plato’s work has been applied to ethical, philosophical, political, managerial, and methodological considerations. Through a brief reading of Protagoras , we highlight the ambiguity of Plato’s dialogical approach to contemporary business ethicists. We then close with Plato’s Ion , indicating difficulties in initiating such dialogues.

KeywordsClassical Philosophy and Thought; Ethics in Business and Education; Philosophical Ethics and Theory; Business ethics; Dialogical self; Ambiguity; Quality of Life Research; Reading (process); Sociology; Business history; Work (physics); Epistemology; Engineering ethics; Close reading; Business studies; Public relations; Philosophy of business; Business model; Business environment; Business analysis; Business management; Management
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldskeywords

750. The eternal adolescent? An interdisciplinary meta-narrative review of African business history

Business History 2026-02-20 Available
JournalBusiness History
Publication date2026-02-20
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1080/00076791.2026.2614417
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Stephanie DeckerUniversity of Birmingham; Birmingham Business School, University of Birmingham
Nicolaas StrydomUniversity of Johannesburg; Department of Accountancy, University of Johannesburg
Julia FernandoFilm Independent; Independent scholar
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Research in African business history is not extensive but highly multi-disciplinary. Our review focuses on publications in key business and economic history journals to better understand the research themes favoured by these two disciplines. We follow a systematic review approach by clearly describing our search strategy and developing a definition of the field with explicit criteria for inclusion and exclusion. Through a meta-narrative approach, we demonstrate that small research niches like African business history function as interdisciplinary intersections that are sometimes too small and fragmented to form coherent research questions and agendas, and which have little consistent recognition of research contributions even within the field. We propose that meta-narrative reviewing can develop greater coherence in such interdisciplinary niches and can help to develop research agendas by providing a better overview of what historical sources are available and what gaps can be productively addressed.

KeywordsAfrican history and culture studies; Family Business Performance and Succession; Oral History, Memory, Narrative Analysis; Business history; Function (biology); Field (mathematics); Inclusion (mineral); Sociology; Coherence (philosophical gambling strategy); Engineering ethics; Business model; Business Review; Key (lock); Business practice; Political science; Social science; Epistemology; Comparative historical research; Interpretation (philosophy); Public relations; History; Systematic review; Management science
Matched conceptsBusiness History
Matched fieldstitle

751. Historical Perspectives on Deglobalization's Drivers, Outcomes, and Managerial Responses

Journal of Management Studies 2026-05-11 Available
JournalJournal of Management Studies
Publication date2026-05-11
DOI URLhttps://doi.org/10.1111/joms.70108
Author & affiliation
AuthorAffiliation
Andrew DA SmithUniversity of Birmingham
Heidi TworekUniversity of British Columbia
Marcelo BucheliUniversity of Illinois Urbana-Champaign; University of Illinois at Urbana‐Champaign
Johann FortwengelKing's College London; University of Pretoria
Abstract statusAvailable
Abstract

Abstract The deglobalization process experienced in the early 2020s is not without precedent. This Special Issue leverages business history as a lens to generate new insights and to uncover previously hidden complexities and nuances. Studying previous periods of deglobalization and their varying drivers, outcomes, and responses, the papers in this Special Issue show how firms and individuals are more active in shaping deglobalization processes than commonly assumed, and that deglobalization is a periodical and uneven phenomenon. The Special Issue also points to exciting avenues for future research, not least to more fully understand the antecedents of deglobalization, and under what conditions possible drivers of deglobalization indeed translate into state action causing deglobalization.

KeywordsGlobal Urban Networks and Dynamics; Elite Sociology and Global Capitalism; Regional Economics and Spatial Analysis; Action (physics); Process (computing); State (computer science); Public relations; Political science; Sociology; Management science; Marketing; Business; Engineering ethics; Epistemology; Positive economics; Through-the-lens metering; Knowledge management; Process management; Computer science; Data science

Bernard YeungNew York University; Stern School of Business, New York University, New York, USA; Stern School of Business, New York University#TAB#
Abstract statusMissing
